Approach 3. Pre-defined Representative Exposure Avalanche and Tornado each have unique methods of calculating exposure. For Avalanche, a single exposure value for population, defined by subject matter experts, is pre-determined and assigned to all areas deemed at risk of Avalanche occurrences. For Tornado, an average historical damage area is calculated for each EF-scale grouping sub

subject matter experts, is pre-determined and assigned to all areas deemed at risk of Avalanche occurrences. For Tornado, an average historical damage area is calculated for each EF-scale grouping subtype. For each sub-type, the representative footprint area is multiplied by the average building and population densities of the Census block to find exposure.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-14 5.3.4. DATA AGGREGATION Exposure is calculated at the Census block level and then is aggregated to the Census tract and county level by summing the Census block exposure values within the parent Census tract or parent county (with the exception of Earthquake, which is initially calculated at the Census tract level). Detailed methodologies per hazard type are explained in the hazard type-specific sections of this report (Sections 6 through 23). 5.4. Historic Loss Ratio The Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard type exposure that experiences loss due to a hazard occurrence or the average rate of loss associated with the hazard occurrence. The Historic Loss Ratio is an area-specific estimate of the percentage of the exposed consequence type (building value, population, or agriculture value) expected to be lost due to a single hazard occurrence. In concept, it is the average of the loss rat

f the percentage of the exposed consequence type (building value, population, or agriculture value) expected to be lost due to a single hazard occurrence. In concept, it is the average of the loss ratios associated with past hazard occurrences and is used to estimate the potential impact of a future hazard occurrence. To begin the determination of this value, a Loss Ratio per Basis (event or event-day) is calculated for each historical loss-causing hazard occurrence as the value of the loss divided by the exposed value for each relevant consequence type. A Bayesian credibility analysis is then performed with sets of individual Loss Ratios per Basis at multiple geographic levels (e.g., county, surrounding area, hazard specific regional area, etc.) and community characteristic groupings (e.g., topography, land cover, proximity to target county, urban-rural designations) to better balance Historic Loss Ratio accuracy with geographic precision and community characteristics. The resulting H

., topography, land cover, proximity to target county, urban-rural designations) to better balance Historic Loss Ratio accuracy with geographic precision and community characteristics. The resulting Historic Loss Ratio (by hazard type-consequence type combination) is a Bayesian-adjusted ratio that is the summed weighted average of various geospatial and community characteristic groupings of the consequence Loss Ratio per Basis sets for the hazard type. This resulting Bayesian-adjusted Historic Loss Ratio value— computed for each county-hazard type-consequence type combination— serves as a foundation for estimating the ratio of loss to exposed consequence type value that is expected from a single hazard occurrence. Computation of the Historic Loss Ratio also considers zero-loss hazard occurrences for some hazard types prior to performing the Bayesian credibility spatial modeling analysis. This ensures that Historic Loss Ratio is multiplied by annualized frequency within the Expected Ann

rences for some hazard types prior to performing the Bayesian credibility spatial modeling analysis. This ensures that Historic Loss Ratio is multiplied by annualized frequency within the Expected Annual Loss equation without overinflating the Expected Annual Loss value. Additionally, the Bayesian-adjusted Historic Loss Ratio values are evaluated against historic loss totals to avoid underestimating losses in areas with credible historic data and may be subject to additional smoothing techniques to improve estimates in neighboring counties. 5.4.1. SOURCE DATA The National Risk Index uses multiple sources for its data to calculate Historic Loss Ratios. For documentation of historical hazard event losses, the National Risk Index primarily relies on the Spatial Hazard Event and Loss Database for the United States (SHELDUS) data set. The National Center for Environmental Information Storm Events Database is used to support Historic Loss Ratio

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-15 work with the Tornado and Inland Flood hazard. The National Center for Health Statistics’ Urban- Rural Classification Scheme for Counties is used to determine the time-dependent classification of a county at the date of a hazard event. Source Data: SHELDUS Historical Losses source data provider: Arizona State University (ASU),Spatial Hazard Event and Loss Database for the United States (SHELDUS)13 Arizona State University’s SHELDUS loss data are used to calculate Historic Loss Ratio for most hazard types. SHELDUS provides county-level data that corresponds to nearly all of the hazard types. It also offers a further degree of description by identifying hazard occurrences by peril type as well as the hazard event itself. SHELDUS represents the best available national dataset on building, population, and agriculture losses. However, it is to be understood that the losses reported in SHELDUS often do not capture all losses of a

represents the best available national dataset on building, population, and agriculture losses. However, it is to be understood that the losses reported in SHELDUS often do not capture all losses of an event or underreport impacts from a peril type overall. That is to say, SHELDUS losses should generally be considered a baseline lower bound of historical impacts and calculated values in excess of SHELDUS figures do not inherently imply overestimations of risk. Through its website, Arizona State University provides summary SHELDUS data that aggregates property damage, crop losses, injuries, and fatalities due to a peril or hazard by month, year, and county since 1960. However, Arizona State University allowed unaggregated data collected at the peril occurrence level to be shared with the Federal Emergency Management Agency for the development of the National Risk Index. Much of this data was originally collected by the National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration and published

deral Emergency Management Agency for the development of the National Risk Index. Much of this data was originally collected by the National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration and published in the monthly Storm Data and Unusual Weather Phenomena report, though information may have also been extracted from additional resources. The records have been processed by Arizona State University to enable appropriate spatial aggregation by distributing losses among multiple counties for events with losses reported at the forecast zone or even the state level. For example, in Table 6, a Winter Weather injury is recorded in SHELDUS records as 0.5 for two neighboring counties. Both occurred in the same date range and have the same level of property damage. This implies that the specific county where the injury occurred could not be determined because the reporting covered two counties, so Arizona State University split the injury evenly between them. Source Data: National Center for Health Statistics Historical Losses source data provider: 2023 National Center for Health Statistics Urban-Rural Classification Scheme for Counties, 15 The Bayesian credibility process used to generate Historic Loss Ratios for most hazard types blends a combination of county, surrounding area, hazard-specific regional area, and national loss ratio sets to balance regional variation and reduce statistical noise. However, problems can arise when blending loss ratios across communities with vastly different exposures. For instance, a credible regional/surrounding area average loss ratio could be driven by small

However, problems can arise when blending loss ratios across communities with vastly different exposures. For instance, a credible regional/surrounding area average loss ratio could be driven by small outlier loss events in rural areas with low exposure. These outlier events can then influence the Historic Loss Ratio of a neighboring urban area with very high exposure leading to significant overestimation of Expected Annual Loss. To help address this, counties are temporally divided into two groups: (1) large central metropolitan areas, as defined by the National Center for Health Statistics Urban-Rural Classification Scheme where NCHSURCode = 1 and (2) all other counties (non-Large Central Metropolitan Areas, where NCHSURCode <> 1). National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-19 SHELDUS loss records were acquired for all perils and all counties in the United States. Loss types include property damage, injuries, fatalities, and crop damage. Property damage and crop damage are quantified in nominal dollars as they were reported at the time the loss occurred. The loss records utilized for the Historic Loss Ratio computation of most hazard types range from January 1996 through December 2023 as loss data captured during and after 1996 were deemed to be the most accurately and uniformly collected due to the standardization of collection practices that began in 1995. However, data from January 1960 to December 2023 are used to compute Historic Loss Ratios for two hazard types: Tornado (EF-2 and above) and Volcanic Activity (see Section 19.6 and Section 21.5). Older event record data are also used to identify which counties had ever experienced losses for a specific hazard type, ensuring that these were se

canic Activity (see Section 19.6 and Section 21.5). Older event record data are also used to identify which counties had ever experienced losses for a specific hazard type, ensuring that these were set in the processing database as counties where there was some possibility of the hazard type occurring (see Determining County- Level Possibility of Hazard Occurrence section). Not all perils to which loss is attributed in SHELDUS and National Centers for Environmental Information are included as National Risk Index hazard types. However, all SHELDUS records that attribute loss to at least one National Risk Index hazard type is extracted. Loss records in SHELDUS can attribute losses for a single loss-causing event to multiple perils. For example, losses from a single storm can be attributed to Wind, Hail, Tornado, and Severe Storm/Thunderstorm. In the processing database, these multi-peril occurrence records are expanded to multiple records, each attributing a portion of the total loss to

Wind, Hail, Tornado, and Severe Storm/Thunderstorm. In the processing database, these multi-peril occurrence records are expanded to multiple records, each attributing a portion of the total loss to a single hazard type. The loss reallocation in these cases does not estimate what degree of loss may be due to perils not included in the hazard types, like Severe Storm/Thunderstorm. Instead, a conservative approach is taken that assumes that all economic loss is due to National Risk Index hazard types. Loss reallocation for each relevant consequence type is based on comparisons between the typical loss caused by each hazard type (see Table 9). To arrive at these percentages, loss attributed to a single hazard type was aggregated across the 1996-2023 period of record for each consequence type, and this loss was compared to the aggregated loss of the other hazard types within the combination as a portion of the combined loss of all hazards in the combination. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-20 Past losses occurring in counties that have been dissolved are included in SHELDUS data as well. These counties are flagged in the SHELDUS data as all records are assigned the name of the county (County FIPS code) at the time the loss was reported. SHELDUS provided a county table that includes the date range when each county definition was applicable. Most changes are due to renaming, a change in the county FIPS code, or the absorption of one county by another. More complex boundary changes necessitate additional processing. The Historic Loss Ratio Methodology attempts to reapportion loss from these dissolved counties to their present-day equivalents if the loss occurred during the period of record for a particular hazard type. The United States Census Bureau maintains a list of Substantial Changes to Counties and County Equivalent Entities 17 that was used to map the dissolved counties to their present-day equivalents. Mos

The United States Census Bureau maintains a list of Substantial Changes to Counties and County Equivalent Entities 17 that was used to map the dissolved counties to their present-day equivalents. Most of these counties were completely absorbed by new or existing counties, and the economic loss of the dissolved county could be 100% reallocated to its present-day equivalent (see Table 10). If a county was dissolved into two or more new or existing counties, the population of the county at the time it was dissolved was compared to the population of the present-day counties to estimate the proportion of loss that should be attributed to each present-day county. The exception to this rule is the reapportionment of the Yellowstone National Park county-equivalent. Loss allocation was divided roughly according to land area reapportionment because the permanent population of the national park is so low. Building Building loss is defined as the SHELDUS or National Centers for Environmental Information reported damage to property caused by the hazard occurrence in dollars (inflation-adjusted to 2022 dollars). In the calculation of Historic Loss Ratios, property loss is treated as ana

tal Information reported damage to property caused by the hazard occurrence in dollars (inflation-adjusted to 2022 dollars). In the calculation of Historic Loss Ratios, property loss is treated as analogous to the building values recorded in Hazus 6.0. However, SHELDUS property damage can include other types of property, like vehicles or infrastructure, which would not be reported in the Census data used by Hazus to estimate building value. This is a caveat to consider when working with the data. SHELDUS and Hazus data remain the best available estimates of loss and value that could be utilized. Population Population loss is defined as the SHELDUS or National Centers for Environmental Information reported number of fatalities and injuries caused by the hazard occurrence. To combine fatalities and injuries for the computation of population loss value, an injury is counted as one-tenth (1/10) of a fatality. Note that for the Cold Wave and Heat Wave hazards, additional Historic Loss Ratio

ies and injuries for the computation of population loss value, an injury is counted as one-tenth (1/10) of a fatality. Note that for the Cold Wave and Heat Wave hazards, additional Historic Loss Ratio values are independently generated for both fatality and injury losses.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-22 Agriculture Agriculture loss is defined as the SHELDUS or National Centers for Environmental Information reported damage to crops and livestock caused by the hazard occurrence in dollars (inflation- adjusted to 2022 dollars). SHELDUS also tracks crop indemnity payments for United States Department of Agriculture-insured crop loss; however, the total crop/livestock damage value was considered to be more inclusive, and the crop indemnity data are not used. 5.4.3. LOSS RECORD FORMATTING The nature and structure of the source loss data requires that some pre-processing and re- formatting of the data within the database to ensure that all historical hazard occurrences are structured appropriately for inclusion in the Historic Loss Ratio calculations, including (1) per-basis record expansion; (2) single-day, timeframe, or consecutive-day aggregation of the SHELDUS and National Centers for Environmental Information loss records; a

atio calculations, including (1) per-basis record expansion; (2) single-day, timeframe, or consecutive-day aggregation of the SHELDUS and National Centers for Environmental Information loss records; and (3) the insertion of records representing zero-loss hazard occurrences. The processes are described in more detail below. Loss Record Expansion to per Basis Records A series of manipulations of the SHELDUS and National Centers for Environmental Information hazard occurrence records are performed to adapt the data for use. For hazards in which the occurrence basis is event-day, records of hazard events that span multiple days have their loss split evenly into a single record per day. For example, the January 2009 Ice Storm event (peril “Ice”) in Table 11 lasted three days. The basis of Ice Storm occurrences is the event-day as this definition better captures the variability in duration for Ice Storm events. Without the resolution of knowing which event-day the damage occurred on, the los

torm occurrences is the event-day as this definition better captures the variability in duration for Ice Storm events. Without the resolution of knowing which event-day the damage occurred on, the loss is divided among the days so that each event-day record has an equal portion of the total loss (see Table 12). Having a record for each hazard occurrence per basis unit better supports the process of determining loss ratio averages and variance. For some hazard types, a cap on the number of days to which a single occurrence could be expanded was set (see Table 14) to prevent certain errors in the date fields from propagating. The rest of the days over the cap value are discarded. Loss Record Aggregation of per Basis Records The Historic Loss Ratio Methodology assumes that multiple reports of loss that occur in the same county during the same date range and are due to the same hazar

Record Aggregation of per Basis Records The Historic Loss Ratio Methodology assumes that multiple reports of loss that occur in the same county during the same date range and are due to the same hazard type are classified as part of the same hazard occurrence. For event-day based hazards, following the date expansions process described above, multiple loss-causing records occurring on the same day are replaced by a single record with the summed losses for each consequence type. For example, the two Hail event records from Table 11 (peril “Hail”) that both occurred on 5/2/2003 are aggregated into a single record in Table 13. This single-day timeframe aggregation ensures that a single-day recorded loss occurring within the date range of a multiple-day recorded loss is treated as the same event-day as one of the days within the multiple-day event. Some event-based hazard types use timeframe aggregation to replace multiple loss-causing records (occurring in the same county with the same St

nt-day as one of the days within the multiple-day event. Some event-based hazard types use timeframe aggregation to replace multiple loss-causing records (occurring in the same county with the same Start and End Date combination) with a single loss record with the summed losses for each consequence type. For example, the two event records from Table 11 where the peril is Wildfire have the same Start and End Date combination. These events are aggregated into a single record in Table 13. This addresses instances where SHELDUS reports damages impacting different areas of the United States for the same multi-day event. For a few event-based hazard types, a consecutive-day aggregation takes place in which loss records that occur in the same county on the same or consecutive days are combined into a single loss record with the summed loss. For example, the two multi-day Hurricane event records from Table 11 (peril “Tropical Storm” and “Storm Surge”) that occurred over consecutive days from J

to a single loss record with the summed loss. For example, the two multi-day Hurricane event records from Table 11 (peril “Tropical Storm” and “Storm Surge”) that occurred over consecutive days from June 12, 2006 to June 14, 2006 are aggregated into a single record in Table 13. This aggregation allows loss records that are due to the same loss-causing events to be logically combined so that each occurrence’s loss ratio is accurately computed. The definition of the Historic Loss Ratio exposure variable in the loss ratio per basis formula does not always match the definition of the exposure factor utilized in the Expected Annual Loss formula. For hazard types that can occur almost anywhere or affect large geographic areas, the Historic Loss Ratio exposure is the ent

ition of the exposure factor utilized in the Expected Annual Loss formula. For hazard types that can occur almost anywhere or affect large geographic areas, the Historic Loss Ratio exposure is the entire county’s building value, population, or agriculture value. Hazard types that only occur in certain susceptible zones, such as floodplains and tsunami inundation zones, use the Historic Loss Ratio exposure value associated with those susceptible zones. Tornado Historic Loss Ratio exposure is defined by the area footprint of specific historical Tornado paths. Avalanche is a unique case that requires the use of default exposure population values (described in Section 6.3). Specific methods of determining Historic Loss Ratio exposure in the Loss Ratio per Basis calculation are found in the Historic Loss Ratio section for each hazard type. For example, Lightning may strike with a high frequency but have few loss-causing occurrences. SHELDUS does not record events in which no loss was reported. In an effort to capture zero-loss hazard occurrences, a coun

tning may strike with a high frequency but have few loss-causing occurrences. SHELDUS does not record events in which no loss was reported. In an effort to capture zero-loss hazard occurrences, a count of historical occurrences is estimated from hazard source data and compared to a count of loss-causing events from SHELDUS and the National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database. A county-level annual rate from the hazard source data is calculated as the count of total hazard occurrences divided by the hazard’s period of record. This rate is then multiplied by the number of years in the SHELDUS period of record to estimate an expected hazard occurrence count. When more occurrences are estimated by the hazard historical occurrence source than SHELDUS or the National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database, a number of zero-loss records are inserted into the set of Loss Ratio per Basis to make up the difference between historical occurrences and lo

ers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database, a number of zero-loss records are inserted into the set of Loss Ratio per Basis to make up the difference between historical occurrences and loss-causing events from SHELDUS so that the counts for both metrics are equal. Computing loss ratio averages and variances without including the zero-loss records produces markedly different results than when they are included. For example, a county with 100 historical Lightning strikes may only have two loss-causing events, one causing $40,000 in damage to buildings and the other causing $60,000. If the building exposure value is $10 million, the loss

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-28 ratios for each loss-causing event would be 0.004 and 0.006, respectively. If only the Loss Ratio per Basis for two loss-causing occurrences were considered, the average would be 0.005. Including the 98 Lightning strikes that did not result in loss lowers the average to 0.0001, a more accurate approximation of the average Lightning strike’s impact on the county as not every Lightning strike is a loss-causing occurrence. The outputs of the Loss Ratio per Basis calculation (see Equation 10) and all corrective record insertion are stored in the Loss Ratio per Basis table within the processing database, and are then used to compute Bayesian metrics and calculate the weighting factors that are applied to find the hazard type Bayesian-adjusted Historic Loss Ratio for each consequence type for the county. HISTORIC LOSS RATIO CALCULATION METHODOLOGY A county Historic Loss Ratio could be computed as the average of the individual hazard occurrence loss rates (referred to here as Loss Ratio per Basis) for a given hazard-consequence type combination. However, a county Historic Loss Ratio cannot be calculated in these simple terms and be considered accurate. Many counties that have not experienced a loss-causing hazard occurrence during the time period captured from SHELDUS may be in close proximity to counties that share similar characteristics and have experienced loss to the hazard type. For example, it may be inaccurate to say that a county’s likely loss ratio to Hurricane is zero just because it has not experienced a l

ar characteristics and have experienced loss to the hazard type. For example, it may be inaccurate to say that a county’s likely loss ratio to Hurricane is zero just because it has not experienced a loss-causing Hurricane occurrence during the 28-year window, especially if it borders counties that have experienced loss to Hurricanes. A better approximation of the Historic Loss Ratio is achieved by applying a Bayesian spatial weighting matrix to smooth the loss ratio data spatially and ensure that the Historic Loss Ratio is represented in a rational way without allowing anomalous hazard occurrences to distort the data. To implement Bayesian credibility weighting, loss ratio averages and variances need to be computed for various sets of Loss Ratio per Basis. These Loss Ratio per Basis sets can be defined by both a geographic and a county characteristic component. The goal in the creation of a Loss Ratio per Basis set is to construct a group of loss ratios that have elements of commonalit

an be defined by both a geographic and a county characteristic component. The goal in the creation of a Loss Ratio per Basis set is to construct a group of loss ratios that have elements of commonality/similarity at several levels: county, surrounding areas, 19 hazard specific regional, and national. These geographic levels define which spatial grouping (or set) of Loss Ratios per Basis are used to calculate the average and variance values. The county-level grouping includes all Loss Ratios per Basis for the county, the surrounding area groupings include Loss Ratios per Basis for all counties that intersect a target county’s buffered area, the regional grouping includes Loss Ratios per Basis for all counties within the defined region, and national includes all Loss Ratios per Basis. Loss Ratio per Basis Sets In the National Risk Index, these groups of similarly characterized counties are referred to as Loss Ratio per Basis Sets. An unlimited number of Loss Ratio Basis Sets can be creat

atio per Basis Sets In the National Risk Index, these groups of similarly characterized counties are referred to as Loss Ratio per Basis Sets. An unlimited number of Loss Ratio Basis Sets can be created by choosing different geographic and desired area characteristics to reference. For example, Loss Ratio per Basis Sets can be created by utilizing urban/rural designation, topography or land cover, proximity, and 19 Two surrounding area definitions are implemented for each county using a buffered area around their centroid with a radius of 115km and 208km, respectively. See the Intersection section for more information on the use of buffered areas to represent a county average.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-30 other characteristics, in order to better understand what a “similar” county is. Loss Ratio per Basis Set definitions are made up of Bayesian Level and National Center for Health Statistics Urban-Rural Code Filter settings and applied to hazard-consequence type combination. Each of these settings can be thought of as a condition for the inclusion of a specific loss ratio record into the set. Bayesian Levels Bayesian levels are geographic definitions that represent a shape or boundary, such as a state or custom-defined area, and serve as a selector for the counties to be associated with the Bayesian level. Bayesian levels can be generally characterized as representing the county, surrounding areas, regional, and national. For the surrounding area category, two different “Neighbor Counties” Bayesian Levels were utilized; one with the focus county buffered by 115km and the other by 208km. Note that each county in the country h

e surrounding area category, two different “Neighbor Counties” Bayesian Levels were utilized; one with the focus county buffered by 115km and the other by 208km. Note that each county in the country has its own Neighbor Counties shape, see Figure 8 for an example. Figure 8: Exam ple of Neighbor County Based on Sets For the regional category, several hazard-specific Bayesian Levels were utilized, each representing county grouping relevant to the hazard. For more information on the hazard-specific Bayesian levels and their associated maps, please see the Historic Loss Record subsections for each hazard (Sections 6 through 23). Additionally in the regional category it was desired to have a regional definition for geographical groupings larger than states but smaller than the nationwide grouping was required. Because the Federal Emergency Management Agency has a pre-existing definition of regions that is logical and groups states by similar geographical and climatological characteristics,

ng was required. Because the Federal Emergency Management Agency has a pre-existing definition of regions that is logical and groups states by similar geographical and climatological characteristics, a decision was made to modify the existing region definition rather than create new region definitions.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-31 Thus, Historic Loss Ratio region definitions for specific hazard types are derived from the Federal Emergency Management Agency administrative region definitions, with the only difference being that Federal Emergency Management Agency Regions 1, 2, and 3 are merged to form a region that is closer in size to that of the other regions, and the Caribbean Territories are given their own region (see Figure 9). Figure 9: Historic Loss Ratio Region Definitions. Filter (County Attribute Filters) Filters are applied to further segment the sets defined by Bayesian levels. Filters use additional county attributes to subdivide the counties within a Bayesian level. The National Risk Index utilized a National Center for Health Statistics Urban-Rural Classification Scheme Urban-Rural Filter definition, see Section 5.4.1. Conceptually for a hazard-consequence type-Bayesian level combination, the Loss Record per Basis Set will contain all l

an-Rural Classification Scheme Urban-Rural Filter definition, see Section 5.4.1. Conceptually for a hazard-consequence type-Bayesian level combination, the Loss Record per Basis Set will contain all loss ratio records associated with the Bayesian level. If a Filter is applied to the Bayesian level, then those Loss Ratios per Basis are further refined to only include those that meet that filter’s specifications – reducing the number of Loss Ratios per B that are used in Historic Loss Ratio calculations.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-32 Set Usage Threshold Usage thresholds are established for determining eligibility of a specific Loss Ratio per Basis set to be included in a specific county’s Historic Loss Ratio calculation. These “Set Usage Thresholds” are established as either a record count or percentage of overall number of Loss Records per Basis that is converted to a record count at the time of evaluation and help to ensure that a minimum number of Loss Records per Basis records are available to be mathematically valid. Set Usage Thresholds can be used in three different modes: 1. Total Record Count Mode – All Loss Record per Basis records in the set are counted regardless of loss ratio value. 2. Non-Zero Record Count Mode – Only Loss Ratio per Basis records with a non-zero loss ratio value are counted. 3. Non-Zero-Padded Record Count Mode – Excludes Loss Ratios per Basis created by the zero- padding process, which simulates losses for non-reporting e

rds with a non-zero loss ratio value are counted. 3. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-37 This resulting Bayesian-adjusted Historic Loss Ratio value, computed for each county-hazard type- consequence type combination, serves as a prediction of the ratio of loss to exposed consequence type value that is expected from a single hazard occurrence. When multiplied by the annualized frequency of hazard occurrence in an area and the consequence type value exposed to the hazard type, the Historic Loss Ratio enables the estimation of a community’s Expected Annual Loss for that consequence type and hazard type. Ex-Post Back Testing and Geographic Smoothing Refinements When Bayesian-adjusted Historic Loss Ratio values are computed for a given hazard and consequence type, an initial set of county level Expected Annual Loss values are calculated and compared with their corresponding annualized losses reported in the SHELDUS period of record. If a given county’s Expected Annual Loss is less than its corresponding annualized S

es are calculated and compared with their corresponding annualized losses reported in the SHELDUS period of record. If a given county’s Expected Annual Loss is less than its corresponding annualized SHELDUS losses, then a new Historic Loss Ratio value is computed by solving for the combination of Bayesian weights that minimizes the absolute difference between the two. This back testing refinements was implemented to mitigate instance where Bayesian credibility weighting may underestimate losses in comparison to our historic loss data. If back testing results in an increase in one or more Historic Loss Ratio values, then a smoothing refinement is applied to neighboring counties if the back-test adjusted Historic Loss Ratio is greater than the national median. This refinement uses an exponential decay function over a 312km radii of influence around the back-test adjusted focus county to smooth out nearby county Historic Loss Ratio values by mean-adjusting them upward subject to predefine

tial decay function over a 312km radii of influence around the back-test adjusted focus county to smooth out nearby county Historic Loss Ratio values by mean-adjusting them upward subject to predefined constraints. 23 To prevent double counting, counties that lie within the radii of influence of more than one back-tested focus county are assigned a weighted average adjustment based upon their relative distance from each source. Hurricane Regression Historic Loss Ratios for Hurricane are calculated using multiple regression. The model regresses county level sociogeographic information on county level mean loss ratios and fitted values are assigned to each county for which a hurricane hazard event is possible. For more information on how Historic Loss Ratio estimates are determined for Hurricane see Section 13.6 Hurricane Historic Loss Ratio. Historic Loss Ratio Inheritance The county Bayesian-adjusted Historic Loss Ratio is inherited by the Census blocks and Census tracts within the parent county when used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations, as in Equation 14. 23 To prevent adverse impacts of smoothing adjustments on Expected Annual Loss, an Expected Annual Loss influence cap was implemented smoothing adjustments which set the maximum allowable increase in Expected Annual Loss to a county as a result of Historic Loss Ratio smoothing equal to the ex-ante national mean Expected Annual Loss for the hazard and consequence type combination. Additionally, National Center for Health Statistics urban-rural county classifications were used to prevent focus counties from influencing nearby counties more urban than themselves.

LIMITATIONS AND ASSUMPTIONS IN HISTORIC LOSS RATIO METHODOLOGY Several factors are not entirely accounted for in the calculation of Historic Loss Ratio. Certain processes, such as Bayesian credibility adjustments, attempt to correct some of these limitations. This section addresses some of the assumptions that are intrinsic within the current methodology and how these can limit the accuracy of the calculation. Evaluating historical economic loss from SHELDUS over a relatively brief period of time and comparing it to a static Historic Loss Ratio exposure value does not acc

it the accuracy of the calculation. Evaluating historical economic loss from SHELDUS over a relatively brief period of time and comparing it to a static Historic Loss Ratio exposure value does not account for changes in development patterns over these years. For example, a hazard occurrence in 1995 may have a low Historic Loss Ratio when its loss is compared to its 2022 Hazus-derived exposure value; however, because of increased development and population influx over the years, its Historic Loss Ratio would be much higher if the same loss were compared to the actual 1995 exposure value. There is an inherent assumption in the methodology that all buildings, population, and agriculture exposed to the hazard are static in economic value and quantity over the data period. Additionally, the SHELDUS loss values are inflation-adjusted to 2022 dollars, and Hazus-derived exposure values are also expressed in 2022 valuations. Since the Historic Loss Ratio calculation is based on historical occur

LDUS loss values are inflation-adjusted to 2022 dollars, and Hazus-derived exposure values are also expressed in 2022 valuations. Since the Historic Loss Ratio calculation is based on historical occurrences, it does not project reductions due to enhanced mitigation efforts and improved building standards that have changed over time (i.e., a seawall being built after a destructive flooding occurrence may reduce the damage caused by subsequent flooding occurrences). Characterizing agriculture losses from occurrences is complex and can vary based on a number of factors, including supply and demand, substitution effects, crop rotation, and seasonality. The simplified Historic Loss Ratio calculations use crop and livestock distribution and values based on agriculture data from CropScape and the Census of Agriculture.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-39 There are many cases where the geographic precision of the recorded loss is imperfectly captured in hazard occurrence reports from National Weather Service and other sources of SHELDUS data. The regional reporting data used to compile SHELDUS may mention multiple counties for a loss-causing occurrence. In these cases, the loss is spread equally over the counties where the hazard occurred, though the loss may have occurred only in one county. Also, loss may only occur in a portion of the county, yet the Historic Loss Ratio will apply to the entire county due to loss not being recorded with any granularity below the county level. 5.5. Validating Expected Annual Loss Estimates to Historical Losses The variety of hazard types and source data presents a significant challenge to provide accurate and meaningful results for the variety of potential lenses through which the results may be viewed, such as:  Hazard type Expected Annu

ource data presents a significant challenge to provide accurate and meaningful results for the variety of potential lenses through which the results may be viewed, such as:  Hazard type Expected Annual Loss rankings within a county  County Expected Annual Loss rankings within a hazard type  County Expected Annual Loss rankings across all hazard types  Hazard type Expected Annual Loss rankings all counties In an atte mpt to validate the Expected Annual Loss, historical losses from SHELDUS and the National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database for the period from 1996 to 2023 were aggregated for the United States for each hazard type and divided by the period of record (28 years) to give a rough nationwide annualized loss estimate.24 This value was compared to the aggregated Expected Annual Loss estimate for its corresponding hazard type. All but 4 (Earthquake, Inland Flooding, Tsunami, and Volcanic Activity) of the hazard type Expected Annual Losses are within

ggregated Expected Annual Loss estimate for its corresponding hazard type. All but 4 (Earthquake, Inland Flooding, Tsunami, and Volcanic Activity) of the hazard type Expected Annual Losses are within a factor of 2 of the experienced historical losses. When evaluating the historical record, losses for some hazard types from the recent past are not representative of their potential future impacts. For example, Earthquakes and Volcanic Activity have the potential to impact high-value urban areas in ways not observed in the SHELDUS period of record. The data and methods used to calculate Expected Annual Losses for these hazard types account for the (albeit small) probability that such outlier events may occur. For this reason, Earthquake and Volcanic Activity Expected Annual Losses are significantly higher than their historical losses provided by SHELDUS. These exceptions aside, a relatively high level of agreement between the calculated Expected Annual Loss and the historical loss records

cantly higher than their historical losses provided by SHELDUS. These exceptions aside, a relatively high level of agreement between the calculated Expected Annual Loss and the historical loss records serves as an indication that the estimated annual hazard loss is fairly aligned with actual recorded historical loss. 24 For Cold Wave, the historic loss data were aggregated from the National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database for 1996 to 2019 and divided by the 27-year period of record.

Expected Annual Loss Rate The three primary types of results provided by the National Risk Index dataset are Expected Annual Loss, Social Vulnerability, and Community Resilience (see Section 3.2 Values, Scor es and Ratings). In addition to those, the National Risk Index provides a set of secondary Expected Annual Loss- related results, called Expected Annual Loss rates, which are calculated from the basic risk components and Expected Annual Loss factors. These Expected Annual Loss rates are designed to reflect the average expected annual percentage loss for the building value, population, and agriculture value within a community. They provide relative natural hazard intensities regardless of the community’s exposure value enabling the comparison of expected losses that are controlled for community size. First, Agriculture Expected Annual Loss Rates tend to be significantly higher than Population or Building Expected Annual Loss Rates because of relatively high Historic Loss Ratios for agriculture compared to the other consequence types. UNDERSTANDING EXPECTED ANNUAL LOSS RATES Expected Annual Loss Rate metrics are calculated as decimals but can be effectively understood and communicated as the fraction of a value (whether buildings, people, or agriculture) that could be expected to be lost annually on average. In the National Risk Index, Expected Annual Loss Rates are expressed as decimals and can be interpreted in the following manner:  A building Expected Annual Loss Rate of 0.00013 is equivalent to an expectation of $1 out of every $7.7K of building value lost to natural hazards annually on average.  A population Expected Annual Loss Rate of 0.000043 is equivalent to an expectation of 1 out of 23K people dying from natural hazards annually on average.  An agriculture Expected Annual Loss Rate of 0.008 is equivalent to an expectation of $1 out of every $125 of agriculture value lost to natural hazards annually on average. In each of the example

ge.  An agriculture Expected Annual Loss Rate of 0.008 is equivalent to an expectation of $1 out of every $125 of agriculture value lost to natural hazards annually on average. In each of the examples above, it is critical to understand the basis upon which the values are calculated. First, they are expected rates, meaning that they are based on models that cannot predict future losses. Second, they are average rates, meaning that actual annual experience may deviate significantly from the projected rates (e.g., some years may experience 10 times the rate, while others may have no losses). Finally, they are annual rates, meaning that they apply to only a 1- year period. A 10-year period would have 10 times the average expected loss. 5.6.4. CALCULATING EXPECTED ANNUAL LOSS Expected Annual Loss is calculated using a multiplicative equation that considers the consequence risk factors of natural hazard exposure, Historic Loss Ratio, and the likelihood risk factor of natural hazard annuali

Loss is calculated using a multiplicative equation that considers the consequence risk factors of natural hazard exposure, Historic Loss Ratio, and the likelihood risk factor of natural hazard annualized frequency for 18 hazard types. The Expected Annual Loss value for each consequence type is calculated by multiplying the exposure value of an area by the estimated annualized frequency and the Historic Loss Ratio (see Equation 21). See Section 5 Expected Annual Loss Factors for further explanation of these Expected Annual Loss factors and how they are computed. Expected Annual Loss Values are computed at the Census block level (or the Census tract level for some hazard types) for each relevant consequence type and summed up to produce a total Expected Annual Loss for each hazard type. A composite Expected Annual Loss is also summed from all hazard type Expected Annual Loss Values for the community (see Equation 21).

The resulting transformed Values are then min-max normalized (0.00 – 100.00 scale) to produce an Expected Annual Loss score for each hazard type (see Equation 22). The composite Expected Annual Loss Score is calculated using the same cube root transformation and min-max normalization process shown in Equation 22. County and Census tract Expected Annual Loss Scores were classified into five qualitative categories, from “Very Low” to “Very High,” using k- means clustering (see Section 3.2 Values, Scores and Ratings). ANALYTICAL TECHNIQUES Arriving at a dollar value representing the Expected Annual Loss due to each of the 18 hazard types for every county and Census tract in the United States requires multiple analytical techniques utilized across all hazard types to ensure the most accurate and consistent representation of Expected Annual Loss. Processing Database To support the processing of the National Risk Index, dedicated SQL Server and PostgreSQL database environments were established. Using a relational database with spatial capabilities to store and analyze each dataset used to comp

he National Risk Index, dedicated SQL Server and PostgreSQL database environments were established. Using a relational database with spatial capabilities to store and analyze each dataset used to compute the National Risk Index’s Values and Scores provides a variety of benefits. The database allows for computational efficiencies when calculating the factors of the Expected Annual Loss for more than 11 million Census blocks in the United States. Grouping and aggregation functions are used to easily aggregate these Values into the Census tract- and county-level Values. Implementation of methodologies in stored procedures and functions allows for the application and adaptation of complex business logic and spatial analysis. The processing database also makes quality control easier by allowing complex calculations to be processed in steps, with the output for each step accessible in its own table. Records for each Census block are checked to identify outliers and any potential problems wit

complex calculations to be processed in steps, with the output for each step accessible in its own table. Records for each Census block are checked to identify outliers and any potential problems with the methodology or algorithms. Additionally, repeatable processes are modified and run in smaller portions, cutting down on processing time as methodology is adapted. For example, a change in source data for a hazard type only requires the replacement of source data tables for that hazard type and for the reprocessing of

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-46 a single hazard type. The processing database also supports version control and allows backups of each version to be stored securely. Most spatial functions, such as buffering and intersection, are performed within the processing database. However, some processes necessitate the additional use of ArcGIS tools and functions. The outputs of these external processes are transferred and stored within the processing database, where they are used to compute the components of the Expected Annual Loss. Geographic/Administrative Layers Expected Annual Loss factors may be calculated at three different administrative layers: Census block, Census tract, and county. The most granular level is the Census block. Where possible, Values are calculated at this level and then aggregated. The source of the boundaries for these layers is the United States Census Bureau’s 2021 TIGER/Line shapefiles 26, except for Connecticut which uses the TIGER

culated at this level and then aggregated. The source of the boundaries for these layers is the United States Census Bureau’s 2021 TIGER/Line shapefiles 26, except for Connecticut which uses the TIGER 2024 boundaries. The shapefiles include United States Territories and some large bodies of water. All spatial layers are transformed into the NAD83 coordinate reference system. The second shapefile, used internally by Federal Emergency Management Agency and referred to as the Federal Emergency Management Agency Mitigation Planning Jurisdiction Layer, 29 is adapted from the Bureau of Indian Affairs geospatial data. 30 This data includes Land Area Representations and Tribal Statistical Area, among other types of tribal areas. If a tribal area covered at least 5% of at least one Census block within a Census tract, a relationship was established between the area and the Census tract. Census tracts that intersect a tribal area, but with less than 5% coverage in a Census block, were visually inspected to ensure that areas of intersection arose out of natural imprecision in the boundaries rather than valid cases of small tribal areas within the Census tract. Following this process, 11 relationship records at the Census tract level and 9 records at the county level were manually included in the final dataset. nal dataset. The work of the National Risk Index to evaluate risk at the county and Census tract level has not been duplicated to give tribal entity risk. Instead, the National Risk Index provides data to identify the spatial relationships between tribal areas and communities; so, that associated county and Census tract profiles are applied to understand tribal entities' risk and Expected Annual Loss data. Determining County-Level Possibility of Hazard Occurrence Not all hazard types are able to occur in all areas. For example, Coastal Flooding is not able to occur in Kansas, and Avalanches are not able to occur on flat terrain. The National Risk Index logically differentiates areas where a given hazard type is unlikely or has never occurred from areas where that hazard type is not able to occur using a control table in the database that designates where each hazard type is able to occur. This designation is based on counties that intersect past hazard occurrence polygons generated thr

occur using a control table in the database that designates where each hazard type is able to occur. This designation is based on counties that intersect past hazard occurrence polygons generated through spatial processing, have some possibility of occurrence as identified by probabilistic or susceptibility source data, or have a recorded loss due to hazard occurrence. December 2025 5-48 National Risk Index Technical Documentation Base Calculation and Aggregation One of the National Risk Index’s strengths is that it determines the Expected Annual Loss for an area at the lowest geographical level deemed appropriate, predominantly the Census block level. Expected Annual Loss is determined by assessing the combination of a specific community’s annualized frequency and associated consequence if it were to occur (for example, how often Inland Flooding occurs in the area and what buildings, population, and agriculture are potentially affected). For many hazard types, annualized frequency and exposure are highly localized. Modeling the annualized frequency in coordination with its exposure provides the best assessment. The Census block is currently the lowest administrative level at which population and building value data are nationally, consistently, and publicly available. By performing the Expected Annual Loss calculation at the Census block level, the

dministrative level at which population and building value data are nationally, consistently, and publicly available. By performing the Expected Annual Loss calculation at the Census block level, the National Risk Index is more accurately assessing Expected Annual Loss by looking at specific annualized frequency and exposure combinations at the lowest possible resolution. The National Risk Index provides the most relevant aggregations to its users, namely Expected Annual Loss Values at the Census tract and county levels. For all hazard types, Census tract- and county-level exposure and annualized frequency are calculated by aggregating Values from the Census block level. The processing database ensures that the aggregated Expected Annual Loss Values do not exceed the aggregated level’s actual exposure values. Data are also aggregated at the state level. Exposure and Expected Annual Loss Values of all Census blocks within each state are summed to give that state’s Values for each hazard

ual exposure values. Data are also aggregated at the state level. Exposure and Expected Annual Loss Values of all Census blocks within each state are summed to give that state’s Values for each hazard type by consequence types. State-level exposure and Expected Annual Loss aggregation for all hazard types except Earthquake is performed in this way. Earthquake exposure and Expected Annual Loss Values are extracted from the Federal Emergency Management Agency Hazus Earthquake Analysis at the Census tract level and aggregated to state level estimations. Expected Annual Loss Values are provided at the state level for each hazard-consequence type combination (i.e., all 18 hazard types for building, population, and agriculture; including Composite Risk). Total state building values, population, and agriculture values are set as ceilings on values by consequence type. The sum of the Expected Annual Losses for each hazard type for each relevant consequence type is used to calculate the state’s

and agriculture values are set as ceilings on values by consequence type. The sum of the Expected Annual Losses for each hazard type for each relevant consequence type is used to calculate the state’s national Expected Annual Loss percentile using a cumulative distribution function to determine the state’s relative Expected Annual Loss. Predictably, this statistic places more populated states with higher building values in the highest percentiles while small and sparsely populated states are in the lower percentiles. State level Risk Index Scores and Ratings are not currently supplied in the dataset. The Social Vulnerability measure from the Census and the FEMA modified version of the Baseline Resilience Indicators for Communities index, the base for Social Vulnerability and Community Resilience components, respectively, are only provided at the Census tract and/or county level. It was determined that deriving meaningful state level representations of these values was out of the curren

lience components, respectively, are only provided at the Census tract and/or county level. It was determined that deriving meaningful state level representations of these values was out of the current scope of the National Risk Index; so, state-level Risk Index Scores are not calculated within the dataset. Hazard type annualized frequency and Historic Loss Ratios are also not calculated at the state level.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-49 Representation of Hazard Types as Spatial Polygons Expected Annual Loss factors for each hazard type are derived from one or more sources of spatial hazard information. This can include identified hazard-susceptible zones, spatiotemporal records of past hazard occurrences, and countywide records of economic loss due to a hazard occurrence. The format of spatial source data varies by hazard type. Annualized frequency and exposure calculations typically require spatiotemporal records of past hazard occurrences or probabilistic modeling. Necessary conversions to prepare for intersection with reference layers are performed with PostGIS spatial operations in the PostgreSQL database environment. The most common method of hazard conversion used for calculations is the buffering of points and lines to form polygons. Point and line representations of hazard occurrences or hazard-susceptible zones are buffered by different distances

rsion used for calculations is the buffering of points and lines to form polygons. Point and line representations of hazard occurrences or hazard-susceptible zones are buffered by different distances depending on the hazard type. Point buffers allow for better representation of hazard occurrence coverage or area of possible impact. Path representations, such as those for Tornado and Hurricane, are included in the source data as a series of points with a common identifier (e.g., StormID). These are connected by a line or multi-segmented line. The line is then buffered by a distance depending on the severity of the Tornado, using the Enhanced Fujita (EF-) scale, or Hurricane, using the Saffir-Simpson scale, hazard occurrence. See the spatial processing discussion in the sections for each hazard type (Sections 6 through 23) for more detail on the buffering techniques used. Conversion from raster to polygon vector format is performed by using ArcGIS Pro’s Raster to Polygon conversion tool.

ard type (Sections 6 through 23) for more detail on the buffering techniques used. Conversion from raster to polygon vector format is performed by using ArcGIS Pro’s Raster to Polygon conversion tool. In vector format, attributes from the source raster data are used to filter or select the data needed for methodology calculations for a specific hazard type. Dasymetric Developed Area, and Agricultural Area Census Blocks For more refined estimations of hazard exposure and frequency at the Census block level, the spatial representations of most hazards were intersected with a dasymetric subset of the Census blocks. To create the dasymetric layer, several spatial datasets are utilized as inputs to custom code to isolate developed area within the 2021 TIGER Census block boundaries 31, except for Connecticut which uses the TIGER 2024 boundaries. For the 50 states and the District of Columbia, three input datasets identifying buildings are pixelated and are each spatially intersected with the

r Connecticut which uses the TIGER 2024 boundaries. For the 50 states and the District of Columbia, three input datasets identifying buildings are pixelated and are each spatially intersected with the Census block polygons to establish a building-Census block relationship. These datasets are the Oak Ridge National Lab Building Footprint dataset 32, the Microsoft Bing Building Footprint dataset33, and the United States Army Corps of Engineers’ National Structure Inventory dataset34. 31 United States Census Bureau. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 5-50 These same three building datasets are also each spatially intersected with the water bodies in the 2021 TIGER Areal Hydrography dataset35 to establish a building-water body relationship. Any building footprint polygons or National Structure Inventory points that intersect a water body are filtered out of the input dataset. Census blocks which contain only water are removed from input into the dasymetric processing of the TIGER Census block boundaries. Additional processing ensures that building footprints in Census blocks intersecting 2019 National Land Cover Database raster pixels with the classification Open Water are not included in the dasymetric output layer. For the entire national dataset, all pixels marked for dasymetric inclusion for a Census block are integrated as a union, and then the Census block boundary is used to clip the unioned shape to generate the final product: the Census block dasymetric geometry. The

ic inclusion for a Census block are integrated as a union, and then the Census block boundary is used to clip the unioned shape to generate the final product: the Census block dasymetric geometry. The tract-level dasymetric geometry is an aggregation of the Census block boundaries and the tract area is the summation of Census block dasymetric area within the tract. The area of the dasymetric Census block is considered the developed area of the block. For most hazards, polygons intersect with the dasymetric Census block or Census tract layer to calculate hazard frequency and building and population exposure. Other hazards use the 2019 National Land Cover Database raster files 36 to determine developed area within the Census block. There are five classes of developed areas (Developed, Developed Open Space, and Developed Low, Medium, and High Intensity) and TIGER Census block intersection with pixels with these classifications is used to create the developed area layer. The same process i

ped Open Space, and Developed Low, Medium, and High Intensity) and TIGER Census block intersection with pixels with these classifications is used to create the developed area layer. The same process is used with the classifications Pasture/Hay and Cultivated Crops to create an agricultural area layer. The dasymetric, developed area, and agricultural area vector layers are created using a custom raster-vector intersect tool. Note that, when developed area is referred to in this document, the dasymetric definition of developed area is what is being described. Intersection Determining areas of spatial intersection between hazard occurrences or hazard-susceptible zones and the various levels of reference layers is an essential function used in calculating Expected Annual Loss. The results of these intersections are stored in the processing database and used for multiple purposes. For many hazard types, the quantification of a hazard type’s exposure is done at the Census block level. This r

hese intersections are stored in the processing database and used for multiple purposes. For many hazard types, the quantification of a hazard type’s exposure is done at the Census block level. This requires the computation of intersecting areas of exposure. Figure 12 provides an example of a hazard occurrence shape intersecting a Census block. A custom intersect tool was developed to perform intersections more rapidly between various layers used to calculate hazard exposure and frequency. This tool stores the unique identifiers of the intersecting polygons, the area of intersection, and, if the intersection is a partial overlap of the reference polygon (typically the dasymetric Census block), the resulting intersect polygon in a results table. This tool was used to perform all intersections except the intersections for creating dasymetric, developed, and agricultural area and the intersection of the Wildfire raster with the Census blocks. These were performed using the raster-vector intersect tool. Annualized frequency computations typically involve counting the number of hazard occurrence polygons that intersect the Census block. Widespread hazards, like Hurricanes, often require a larger aggregation layer to represent the annualized frequency of hazard occurrences more accurately. For these hazard types, the intersection is performed with a 49-by-49 km fishnet grid, and the count of the fishnet grid cell is inherited by the Census blocks it encompasses using an area-weighted value when a Census block intersects more than one cell. Fi gure 12: Example of Intersection Between Hazard Occurrence and Census Block The 49-by-49 km grid cell size was used because of analysis that roughly estimated the average Census tract size to be 4,900 m2 (or 70-by-70 m) and the average county size to be 2,500 km2 (or 50-by-50 km), which was reduced slightly to 49-by-49 km to ensure the county size was a multiple of the Census tract size. Though the use of a grid at the average Census tract resolution was discarded, the use of the 49-by-49 km fishnet grid was maintained for the calculation of annualized frequency for widespread hazard types.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 6-1 6. Avalanche An Avalanche is a mass of snow in swift motion traveling down a slope. This section provides an overview of the data and methodology used to estimate risk from Avalanche events. For the Avalanche hazard, an avalanche susceptibility layer was developed using slope and snow depth data to identify areas across the United States that could feasibly experience avalanche events. Historical loss and frequency data for the building and population consequence types were sourced from the Colorado Avalanche Information Center, SHELDUS, and National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s National Climatic Data Center Storm Events Database. Agricultural losses are not considered in the Avalanche Expected Annual Loss calculations. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 6.1. Avalanche Spatial Source Data Susceptible Area Source: 1/3 arc-second Digital

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 6-2 The Natural Resources Conservation Service manages a comprehensive network of manually measured snow courses and automated Snow Telemetry (SNOTEL) monitoring sites throughout the western United States and Alaska. The SNOTEL Network is comprised of 900 automated data collection sites across remote, high elevation mountain watershed areas in the western United States with an additional 90 stations across Alaska. The records for each station are unique to that station, so some stations have daily snow depth records covering more than 50 years, but most of the stations with daily snow depth data have only been active since the mid-1990s. Susceptible Area Source: Northern Hemisphere 0.25 Degree Resolution Machine Learning Snow Depth Data40 The Northern Hemisphere 0.25 degree resolution machine learning snow depth data is a data fusion framework based on Random Forest Regression of snow products from Advanced Microwave Scanning Ra

Data40 The Northern Hemisphere 0.25 degree resolution machine learning snow depth data is a data fusion framework based on Random Forest Regression of snow products from Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer for the Earth Observing System, Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer-2, Global Snow Monitoring for Climate Research, the Northern Hemisphere Snow Depth, ERA-Interim, and Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications, version 2. It incorporates geolocation (latitude and longitude), and topographic data (elevation), as input independent variables. More than 30,000 ground observation sites were used as the dependent variable to train and validate the model in different time periods. This fusion framework resulted in a series of continuous daily snow depth data from 1980 through 2019 over the Northern Hemisphere with a spatial resolution of 0.25° (approximately 27.75 km). Avalanche Spatial Data Processing 6.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Studies of avalanche hazards have indicated that there are two primary factors that lead to potential avalanche events: slope angle and snow depth. 44 While other factors such as land cover and slope aspect play a part in the frequency and size of avalanche events, they are generally considered to be minor factors. According to subject matter experts, Avalanche events are most likely to occur in regions where the terrain slope is

cy and size of avalanche events, they are generally considered to be minor factors. According to subject matter experts, Avalanche events are most likely to occur in regions where the terrain slope is between 30 and 50° and the snow depth is 1 meter or more.45 Slope and snow depth data were overlaid to identify areas which satisfied those two requirements, creating a national susceptibility map for all areas with the potential for an avalanche event, including insignificant events. With the focus being on avalanche events that would have a significant impact on lives and property, the broad scope of the susceptibility map was limited, using the identified locational qualifiers in Section 6.2.5. 6.2.2. SLOPE PROCESSING Historical avalanche release events frequently occur where slope angles are between 30 and 50 degrees; slopes less than 30 degrees are not steep enough to initiate an avalanche release, while those greater than 50 degrees are often too steep for enough snow to accumulate

are between 30 and 50 degrees; slopes less than 30 degrees are not steep enough to initiate an avalanche release, while those greater than 50 degrees are often too steep for enough snow to accumulate for a large avalanche event. Slope data was obtained using the U.S. Geological Survey nationwide 1/3 arc- 43 NOAA, National Weather Service. (2024). NCDC Storm Events Database. The influence of topographic parameters on avalanche release dimension and frequency. The 10-meter digital elevation model was upscaled to produce a 100-meter digital elevation model, from which the slope was calculated using the ArcGIS Pro Surface Parameters tool across the continental United States and Alaska. While a finer-grained analysis was possible, it was determined that the slopes identified on smaller scales included smaller stream valleys and road cuts that were not aerially large enough to produce significant avalanche events. 6.2.3. SNOW DEPTH PROCESSING: CONTINENTAL UNITED STATES Snowfall and snow depth are the other main factors for an avalanche event to occur. While avalanches are primarily known from areas of deep snow coverage, avalanche events can occur any time the snow depth exceeds 1 meter. The SNODAS data was analyzed to obtain the maximum daily snow depth at every grid cell for the entire 18-year history of the dataset. This maximum snow dept

ur any time the snow depth exceeds 1 meter. The SNODAS data was analyzed to obtain the maximum daily snow depth at every grid cell for the entire 18-year history of the dataset. This maximum snow depth data was used to create a maximum snow depth triangular irregular network layer, from which a maximum snow depth raster was created. To minimize snow depth extrapolations amongst distant snow depth points, a 5-km buffer was added to all of the snow depth grid points to create a mask from which to extract the snow depth raster. All raster cells with a snow depth of 1-meter or greater were then extracted from the layer. 6.2.4. SNOW DEPTH PROCESSING: ALASKA SNODAS data were not available for Alaska, so a combination of SNOTEL and Hu et al.’s (2023) 0.25°machine learning-produced daily snow depth data were combined to identify areas with the potential to obtain greater than 1-meter snow depths across Alaska. For each SNOTEL station, a maximum daily snow depth was identified across the entire

h data were combined to identify areas with the potential to obtain greater than 1-meter snow depths across Alaska. For each SNOTEL station, a maximum daily snow depth was identified across the entire history of each station. The SNOTEL data points were supplemented with the 0.25°machine learning-produced daily snow depth data. The machine learning snow depth data is partially produced using passive microwave brightness to determine a semi-global map of daily snow depth. This method is known to underestimate snow depths when actual depths are greater than 1 meter, however since the avalanche threshold is 1 meter, the data can produce depth limits within the boundaries needed for this analysis. The 0.25°machine learning-produced daily snow depth data was contoured at 0.2 meters. The contours were used as “soft” lines with the SNOTEL data being used as “hard” points to create a triangular irregular network surface. This triangular irregular network was translated to a raster and, as with

s were used as “soft” lines with the SNOTEL data being used as “hard” points to create a triangular irregular network surface. This triangular irregular network was translated to a raster and, as with the conterminous United States, the snow depth was then selected for all areas that exceeded 1 meter in depth. 6.2.5. SUSCEPTIBILITY AREA REFINEMENT The historical record was used to identify locations where avalanche events have been known to occur. Outside of these areas, states, counties, and urban areas without previous avalanche hazards were examined for the possibility of future avalanche events. In that regard, midwestern counties and states east of the Rocky Mountains and the Badlands and areas south of Virginia/Kentucky were assumed to have a near-zero chance of an impactful avalanche event. In the Northeastern United States, areas outside of the Appalachian Mountains were considered to have low likelihood of

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 6-5 avalanche events. Susceptible zones across the continental United States were also analyzed where they intercepted urban areas as identified by the Census46. After careful analysis, it was decided that select urban areas should be excluded from the susceptibility layer if they were located within steep river valleys. This was likely a relic of spatial resolution of the data sources versus actual avalanche hazard and primarily impacted urban areas in the Northeastern United States Urban areas in the western states that are located near mountain ranges were deemed potentially at risk. All states and counties identified as unlikely for future impactful avalanche events were removed from the analyses. These states and counties are illustrated in Figure 13. PERIOD OF RECORD Combined, the SHELDUS, Colorado Avalanche Information Center, and National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration databases provide robust avalanche records dating from Jan 01, 1994 through Dec. 31, 2023, resulting in a period of record of 30 years. While the SHELDUS data do extend back to 1960, an analysis of the data shows extremely limited, and sometimes incorrect, event identifications prior to 1994, so a cutoff of 1994 was used for frequency determinations. 6.3.3. HISTORICAL EVENT FREQUENCY Events from the National Climatic Data Center Storm Events Database, Colorado Avalanche Information Center Accident Data, and SHELDUS database were cross checked to ensure only unique events were included. All three of the databases were checked for quality and accuracy of the l

valanche Information Center Accident Data, and SHELDUS database were cross checked to ensure only unique events were included. All three of the databases were checked for quality and accuracy of the locations, as spatial locations occasionally did not match the physical description (i.e., a data point in Washington described an event in Utah). These historical events were then used to determine the frequency of events at the county level based on the number of historical events within that county across the period of record, 30 years. 6.4. Avalanche Exposure 6.4.1. BUILDING Avalanches occur primarily in sparsely populated mountainous areas, so exposure values tend to be low. Avalanche exposure is determined by identifying the Hazus dasymetric shapes that overlap with the Avalanche Susceptibility Zones. The Hazus dasymetric shapes represent the highest likelihood of buildings within the susceptibility zones. 6.4.2. POPULATION While building exposure estimates are straightforward, popula

bility Zones. The Hazus dasymetric shapes represent the highest likelihood of buildings within the susceptibility zones. 6.4.2. POPULATION While building exposure estimates are straightforward, population exposure is difficult since people are typically exposed to avalanches while enjoying extracurricular activities in mountainous backcountries. For population exposure, default county values were identified based on historical fatality and injury records for each physiographic region (Figure 15). These default values are 3, 5, or 10 people per avalanche event based on how frequently people are injured or killed in each region (Table 18). AGRICULTURE Avalanches occur during the winter months at times when temperatures are below freezing so agriculture is typically minimally impacted, and while avalanches have been known to knock over trees, the historical record of avalanche events impacting agricultural orchards is unknown. 6.4.4. COUNTY-LEVEL EXPOSURE ESTIMATION At the county level, the building exposure value is the summation of the building exposure value of all the Cens

nts impacting agricultural orchards is unknown. 6.4.4. COUNTY-LEVEL EXPOSURE ESTIMATION At the county level, the building exposure value is the summation of the building exposure value of all the Census tracts within the county, which is the summation of all exposure values within the Census blocks. The Census tract inherits its population exposure value from the parent county. For example, if a county has a default population exposure value set to 5 people, each tract within that county is also assigned a population exposure value of 5 people. This assumes that those 5 exposed people within that county may at any moment be located in any given Census tract where avalanches are possible. 6.5. Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the number of Avalanche loss-causing occurrences, in events, each year over the period of record (30 years). The Census tract inherits the parent county-level value, and the Census tract value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Avalanche hazard type. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS,47 Colorado Avalanche Information Center, and National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated. ses, SHELDUS data from 1996 through 2023 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio for Building calculations.
