Avalanche Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user

s section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptio ns and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  This process is assumed to identify all areas that are potentially prone to Avalanche events. However, this is just a static snapshot of a very dynamic hazard -- the triggering conditions that could cause an Avalanche event are variable year to year, month to month, and even day to day.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 6-18  This process is known to overestimate some areas where there are steep slopes but minimal potential for impactful Avalanche events, such as along river valleys, and areas in the southern states where no Avalanches are anticipated. While these areas were limited by excluding counties and urban areas known to not have historical Avalanche events, in other areas they are likely to still be prevalent. Further refinement of the identification process is needed to better differentiate these areas.  The current potential Avalanche hazard map does not contain a gradational scale, which would identify areas within this susceptible region that are more or less likely to have Avalanche events. Features such as solar aspect angle and prevailing wind direction are known to have an impact on Avalanche potential. This may also be informed by previous inventories of North American Public Avalanche Danger Scale (NAPADS), which is a syste

evailing wind direction are known to have an impact on Avalanche potential. This may also be informed by previous inventories of North American Public Avalanche Danger Scale (NAPADS), which is a system used by Avalanche forecasters that rates avalanche danger based on the likelihood, size, and distribution of expected avalanches.50F50F51  Even though the current source for historical Avalanche events is more comprehensive than was previously available, the number of Avalanche events is still vastly underreported since the historical data only record impactful events. The most comprehensive historical record from the Colorado Avalanche Information Center only contains records with fatalities and does not include records for injuries or population impact. The location of all the historical events is sometimes not fully accurate, with many records only being able to be identified down to the county level. There are also no indications in the current data of the cause of the Avalanche eve

events is sometimes not fully accurate, with many records only being able to be identified down to the county level. There are also no indications in the current data of the cause of the Avalanche events as natural versus human-induced. With a greater historical record of Avalanche events, including natural events that are known to not have caused a significant impact on people or property, a more nuanced Avalanche hazard identification procedure can be implemented. These additional historical event records may be available from regional and local sources. 51 https://avalanche.org/avalanche-encyclopedia/#north-american-public-avalanche-danger-scale

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 7-1 7. Coastal Flooding Coastal Flooding is when water inundates or covers normally dry coastal land as a result of high or rising tides or storm surges. This section provides an overview of the data and methodology used to estimate risk from Coastal Flooding. The Coastal Flooding hazard utilizes the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s National Flood Hazard Layer, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration High Tide Flooding probability data and Sea, Lake, and Overland Surges from Hurricanes (SLOSH) maximum-of- maximum raster data to estimate annualized losses to coastal flooding and storm surge along the Pacific, Atlantic, and Gulf coastlines. For the Great Lakes region, a distinct methodology using the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment product was employed. The Coastal Flooding hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a su

Management Agency’s Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment product was employed. The Coastal Flooding hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 7.1. Coastal Flooding Spatial Source Data Susceptible Area Source: National Flood Insurance Program, National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL)52 The National Flood Hazard Layer contains several layers depicting flood information, including levee locations, Flood Insurance Rate Map boundaries, and Special Flood Hazard Areas or floodplain polygons. The Special Flood Hazard Area polygons for 1% annual chance and 0.2% annual chance were downloaded in shapefile format for use in the calculation of Coastal Flooding exposure and annualized frequency. Susceptible Area Source: CoreLogic Special Flood Hazard Area Layer The CoreLogic digitized floodplain boundaries supplement the Federal Emergency Manag

stal Flooding exposure and annualized frequency. Susceptible Area Source: CoreLogic Special Flood Hazard Area Layer The CoreLogic digitized floodplain boundaries supplement the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s official digital National Flood Hazard Layer data in areas where only paper Flood Insurance Rate Maps exist. These boundaries have been compiled by CoreLogic through the digitization of existing paper flood maps and the use of legacy paper the Federal Emergency Management Agency products. the Federal Emergency Management Agency has licensed this data from CoreLogic to supplement its National Flood Hazard Layer data while the Federal Emergency Management Agency engages with communities where digital data-coverage gaps exist in the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s National Flood Hazard Layer. 52 National Flood Insurance Program, FEMA. (2025). NFHL [online dataset]. Retrieved from https://www.fema.gov/national- flood-hazard-layer-nfhl.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 7-2 Susceptible Area Source: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Office for Coastal Management, High Tide Flooding (HTF) Probability53 The High Tide Flooding Probability data set provides estimates of the probability of Coastal Flooding events due to high tides for United States coastal regions. The dataset categorizes flooding events across three severity levels: Minor flooding events (1–2-year floods), Moderate flooding events (10- 50 floods), and Major flooding events (100-year floods). For each severity level, there is a GeoTIFF raster layer where each raster cell contains information about the probability of High Tide Flooding at a given location, represented by a number between 0 and 100. Susceptible Area Source: The Federal Emergency Management Agency, Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment The Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment contains Hazus dasymetric developed area polygon features. Each feature has an attribute v

he Federal Emergency Management Agency, Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment The Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment contains Hazus dasymetric developed area polygon features. Each feature has an attribute value that describes an average annual loss rate due to flood hazard associated with a one-story single-family home with a slab-on-grade foundation. The model and framework are built on years of investment in flood hazard information and analysis through the National Flood Insurance Program and Risk Mapping, Assessment, and Planning program. The Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment depicts inland flood losses derived from geographic factors such as elevation, distance to rivers, and relative elevation from a river, combining catastrophe modeling, National Flood Hazard Insurance Program historical data, and actuarial pricing approach. ata, and actuarial pricing approach. This information is used to inform potential annualized loss rates for different building types at the dasymetric Census block level. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 7-3 7.2. Coastal Flooding Spatial Data Processing Coastal spatial processing included numerous complex steps in order to complete Expected Annual Loss calculations. The process uniquely modeled Coastal Flooding exposure by the sub-type of flooding and calculated corresponding annualized frequencies for each flooding sub-type. The sub- types of flooding included in the Coastal Flooding hazard type are:  National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Minor High Tide Flooding Event Area  National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Moderate High Tide Flooding Event Area  National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Major High Tide Flooding Event Area  Special Flood Hazard Area 100-year flood area  Special Flood Hazard Area 500-year flood area  The Federal Emergency Management Agency Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment All Nationa l Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration High Tide Flooding Coastal Flood Frequency rasters w

500-year flood area  The Federal Emergency Management Agency Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment All Nationa l Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration High Tide Flooding Coastal Flood Frequency rasters were converted to polygons and cell values were assigned into 8 event probability/frequency categories. All spatial datasets were first extracted and, if necessary, converted to polygon vector format. National Flood Hazard Layer data were extracted for 1% annual chance coastal floodplains (100- year) and 0.2% annual chance floodplains (500-year) according to their flood-zone sub-type. “V” Zones and “A” Zones with the word “coastal” in the sub-type signify coastal 1% annual chance floodplains and were extracted. Additionally, where National Flood Hazard Layer data were unavailable, flood hazard areas in which the flood zone category begins with “V” were extracted from the CoreLogic data. The National Flood Hazard Layer and CoreLogic selections were combined into a single layer representing S

areas in which the flood zone category begins with “V” were extracted from the CoreLogic data. The National Flood Hazard Layer and CoreLogic selections were combined into a single layer representing Special Flood Hazard Area 100-year flood area. The 0.2% annual chance extract was combined with an extract of “X” Zone with sub-type “AREA WITH REDUCED FLOOD RISK DUE TO LEVEE” to create a Special Flood Hazard Area 500-year flood area. For the Great Lakes Region, a Great Lakes flood area is determined by the extent of all polygons within the Federal Emergency Management Agency Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment with a “Great Lakes Flooding Premium without Fees” greater than 0. By region, the highest available SLOSH raster file was converted into a polygon vector format and intersected with Census block to support the designation as possible for Coastal Flooding (see Section 7.2.1 Determination of Possibility of Hazard Occurrence). 7.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE To di

block to support the designation as possible for Coastal Flooding (see Section 7.2.1 Determination of Possibility of Hazard Occurrence). 7.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE To distinguish between areas with no Coastal Flooding occurrences and those where such occurrences are not deemed possible, a control table was generated to designate which Census blocks have some probability of being impacted by a Coastal Flooding occurrence. Coastal Flooding

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 7-4 was deemed possible in different regions based on different criteria. For the Gulf Coast and southeast Atlantic coast from Texas to North Carolina and Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands, Census blocks that intersected the SLOSH Maximum of the Maximum raster layer for Category 5 storms were deemed possible for Coastal Flooding. For the northeast Atlantic coast from Virginia to Maine, Census blocks that intersected the SLOSH Maximum of the Maximum layer for Category 4 storms were deemed possible for Coastal Flooding. The SLOSH Maximum of the Maximum layer for Category 2 storms was used to determine Coastal Flooding possibility in Southern California, and SLOSH Maximum of the Maximum layer for Category 4 storms was used for Hawai‘i. For American Samoa and Guam, all Census blocks along the coastline were deemed possible. All of Northern Mariana Islands were included as possible. For the Great Lakes, any Census block in a Ce

wai‘i. For American Samoa and Guam, all Census blocks along the coastline were deemed possible. All of Northern Mariana Islands were included as possible. For the Great Lakes, any Census block in a Census tract along the coastline was deemed possible for Coastal Flooding, as well as any Census blocks which intersected a coastal 1% or 0.2% annual chance floodplain in the National Flood Hazard Layer as defined in 7.2. The National Flood Hazard Layer with these criteria were also used to designate a portion of the shoreline of the Great Salt Lake in Utah possible for Coastal Flooding. On the coasts of Alaska, Washington, Oregon, and most of California, Hazus coastal area designations were used to determine Coastal Flooding possibility. (see Figure 19). Coastal Flooding Historic Loss Ratio The Coastal Flooding Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure area that experiences loss due to a Coastal Flooding occurrence, or the average rate of loss associated with a Coastal Flooding occurrence. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Coastal Flooding hazard type. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS57 at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated. The Expected Annual Loss Score represents a community’s national percentile ranking relative to all communities at the same level. Building Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Coastal Flooding Expected Annual Loss for buildings by the total building value of the community. Coastal Flooding Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for

Flooding Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  There are several assumptions in the Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment loss data that result in limitations to these modeled impacts. The first is that Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment assumes all buildings are single-story, single-family homes with a slab-on-grade foundation

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 7-15 that has a replacement cost value of $250,000. Any adjustments made for different occupancy types are estimations and might not fully capture specific vulnerabilities, and foundation types other than slab-on-grade are estimated using generalized damage functions, which may not reflect accurate damage levels. As such, there is potential for underestimation or overestimation of losses due to reliance on generalized damage functions rather than specific empirical data.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 8-1 8. Cold Wave A Cold Wave is a rapid fall in temperature within 24 hours and extreme low temperatures for an extended period. The temperatures classified as a Cold Wave are dependent on the location and defined by the local National Weather Service weather forecast office. The Cold Wave hazard modeling uses Mesonet data to provide impact areas for this hazard that are derived from the National Weather Service’s warnings and advisories. SHELDUS data were used for historical losses for Cold Wave events and generate the Historic Loss Ratios, with data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Compressed Mortality Database used to supplement the SHELDUS mortality data. These are overlaid with the exposure values provided in Hazus 6.0 for Building, Population, and Agriculture for the final Expected Annual Loss. The Cold Wave hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. Fo

Hazus 6.0 for Building, Population, and Agriculture for the final Expected Annual Loss. The Cold Wave hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 8.1. Cold Wave Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Compiling Source: Iowa State University, Iowa Environmental Mesonet58 The National Weather Service is continuously issuing weather alerts based on current weather conditions. 59 Each alert is coded by type of phenomenon and level of significance, (e.g., Watch, Warning, or Advisory) and conceptually can indicate the potential for weather event activities in a specific area. Archived National Weather Service alerts are aggregated, continuously updated, and made available for download in shapefile format by Iowa State University's Iowa Environmental Mesonet. Data include geometry for each alert's issued area and attributes rel

, continuously updated, and made available for download in shapefile format by Iowa State University's Iowa Environmental Mesonet. Data include geometry for each alert's issued area and attributes related to each alert’s severity and phenomena type. Weather alerts are also timestamped with the time of issuance and the time of expiration. A table describing this dataset’s attributes can be found in Appendix C – Mesonet-National Weather Service Weather Event Attribute Description. The geometry shape for each alert record represents the geographic area for which the National Weather Service alert applied. However, the Mesonet shapes are simplified versions of the more detailed National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shape originally associated with the alert record. Because the Mesonet tabular data still retain the reference IDs for the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones, these reference IDs can be used to relate to the more detailed zone associated with each alert recor

tabular data still retain the reference IDs for the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones, these reference IDs can be used to relate to the more detailed zone associated with each alert record. The National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones can be downloaded in shapefile format60 and represent the codified areas for which weather alerts are issued by National Weather Service. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 8-2 Public Forecast Zones shape definitions are predominantly derived from county boundaries. While the Public Forecast Zone boundaries are more refined than those substituted into the Mesonet data, they are not at the same resolution as the current county boundaries derived from Census blocks. Utilizing the Public Forecast Zone shapefile in conjunction with the Public Forecast Zone – County Correlation file, 61 a determination was made as to which Public Forecast Zones have single-county coverage and which are either sub-county zones or made of portions of multiple counties. For perspective, the following approximate distributions of forecast zone composition were found:  70% of the zones are single-county coverage.  20% are cases where a single county is subdivided into multiple zones.  10% are zones that breach parts of multiple contiguous counties. For thos e Forecast Zones covering a single county, the United Sates Censu

s where a single county is subdivided into multiple zones.  10% are zones that breach parts of multiple contiguous counties. For thos e Forecast Zones covering a single county, the United Sates Census Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing 2021 county boundaries (Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing 2024 boundaries for Connecticut) are substituted. Another aspect of the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones is that they can and have changed over time. In the Mesonet data (2005 through 2024), there are many Forecast Zones referenced that do not exist in the current National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shapefile. This occurs when a National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone has been modified in shape, renamed, and/or “retired” from use. Unfortunately, no such archival information is currently available. For cases where the more refined National Weather Service Forecast Zone shape is unavailable, the simplified Mesonet boundary version shape is used. Cold Wave Spatial Data Processing Cold Wave weather event alerts were extracted from the dataset based on the Valid Time Extent Code significance code (SIG field) and the phenomena code (PHENOM or TYPE field) values. Only Warning alerts (SIG = ‘W’) and Advisory alerts (SIG = ‘Y’) of the Phenomena type “Extreme Cold” (EC) or “Wind Chill” (WC) were considered Cold Wave occurrences (see Table 26). To remove unintended errors in spatial results due to the use of the simplified event area shapes contained in the Mesonet data, event areas with a higher resolution version were substituted. This substitution uses the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shape associated with the alert record or, in cases where the forecast zone is for a single county, a better resolution version of the county boundary area. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 8-4 Cold Wave occurrences are measured in event-days as this more accurately represents the variability of Cold Wave event duration.62 To capture this, each native alert record with a duration greater than a single day was replaced with multiple records, one for each day of the original record’s duration. If a Cold Wave event’s duration on any given day is less than 6 hours, then the event is assigned to the day having the greatest duration of the event. This accounts for cases where the event occurs in the late evening and endures for a greater length of time on the next calendar day than on the day the alert was issued. For cases where the event duration is longer, the following logic is used: If a weather event’s duration is greater than 6 hours, assign the event to all days on which 6 or more hours occur. For example, if a 14-hour weather event was issued for 2 AM until 6 PM on January 1, then the event would be assigned to

ter than 6 hours, assign the event to all days on which 6 or more hours occur. For example, if a 14-hour weather event was issued for 2 AM until 6 PM on January 1, then the event would be assigned to January 1. If the alert was issued from 11 PM on January 1 to 1 PM on January 2, then the event would be assigned to only January 2. If the alert was issued from 6 PM on January 1 to 9 AM on January 2, then the event would be assigned to both January 1 and January 2. To illustrate this concept, the Cold Wave event in Table 27 is expanded to create the Cold Wave event- day records in Table 28. Additionally, there are some data quality issues with the Mesonet data. For example, some warnings have an expiration date that is prior to the issue date. In these cases, a single record is used and assigned the issue date. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 8-5 Cold Wave event areas occurring on the same day (Year, Month, Day specific) into one representative event shape was performed. This process results in a single event impact area shape for each day on which a Cold Wave event occurred. These Cold Wave event-day polygons can then be intersected with the Census block polygons to determine Cold Wave exposure and annualized frequency. 8.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Cold Waves can occur almost anywhere in the United States as the definition of a Cold Wave is locally defined by the area’s Weather Forecast Office. For example, a forecast office in Texas may define a Cold Wave differently than a forecast office in New York. Therefore, all counties are deemed possible for Cold Wave occurrence. 8.3. Cold Wave Non-Spatial Data Processing 8.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Cold Wave, in event-days, is computed as the area-weight

ible for Cold Wave occurrence. 8.3. Cold Wave Non-Spatial Data Processing 8.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Cold Wave, in event-days, is computed as the area-weighted sum of distinct Cold Wave event-day polygons that intersect a Census block. Historical event-day counts are also supplied at the Census tract and county levels as the number of distinct Cold Wave event-day polygons that intersect the Census tract and county, respectively. 8.3.2. PERIOD OF RECORD The National Weather Service’s system of recording Watch, Warnings, and Advisories in the 1990s and early 2000s was too difficult for automated processing. In 2005 the Valid Time Extent Code system was implemented, which allowed for the easy automated parsing of alert data. Therefore, National Weather Service weather events data were downloaded for 2005 through 2024. The date range is Nov. 12, 2005 to Nov. 12, 2024, so the period of record for which Cold Wave data are utilized is 19.0 years. 8.4

er Service weather events data were downloaded for 2005 through 2024. The date range is Nov. 12, 2005 to Nov. 12, 2024, so the period of record for which Cold Wave data are utilized is 19.0 years. 8.4. Cold Wave Exposure To identify areas of exposure, the Cold Wave event-day polygons (also referred to as Cold Wave Date Expansions to acknowledge the spatiotemporal processing described in Section 8.2 Cold Wave Spatial Data Processing) are intersected with the Census block polygons within the processing database. The resulting table contains the Cold Wave event-day’s unique identifier, Census block number, and the intersected area in square kilometers (see Table 29). Table 29: Sample Data from the Cold Wave Expansion Census Block Intersection Table ColdwaveDateExpansionID CensusBlock IntersectedAreaKm2 2025 120830011043089 0.0331315054931641

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 8-6 ColdwaveDateExpansionID CensusBlock IntersectedAreaKm2 2025 120830011043090 0.00229587890625 2025 120830011043091 0.00324445764160156 To determine exposure value, the average coverage of a Cold Wave event-day is found by summing the intersected areas for all Cold Wave event-day polygons that intersect the Census block and dividing this sum by the number of intersecting event-day polygons. This is multiplied by the developed area building value density, the developed area population density, and the agriculture area value density of the Census block to model the conservative-case concentration of exposure within the Census block (see Equation 34). These Census block densities have been calculated by dividing the Census block total exposure values (as recorded in Hazus 6.0) by the developed or agriculture land area (in square kilometers). It should be noted that, for a Cold Wave event-day polygon’s intersection with a Census block to be included, the area of the intersection must cover at least 5% of the Census block. This is a spatial modeling technique to correct for the small intersect “slivers” generated by differing versions of county boundary geometry being used. Because the exposure model uses a conservative-case concentration of exposure and a developed area density value, it is possible to mathematically gen

versions of county boundary geometry being used. Because the exposure model uses a conservative-case concentration of exposure and a developed area density value, it is possible to mathematically generate an exposure value that is greater than the total value of the Census block. The Hazus-recorded population and building value and the Census of Agriculture-reported crop and livestock value for the Census block are considered ceilings on exposure. For example, if the calculated exposed building value exceeds the Hazus-recorded building value, then the Hazus-recorded building value is used as the building exposure value for the Census block. 8.4.1. EXPOSURE AGGREGATION To calculate exposure at the Census tract level, the exposure values for each Census block within the Census tract are summed. To calculate exposure at the county level, the exposure values for each Census block within the county are summed (see Equation 35).

Cold Wave Annualized Frequency The number of recorded Cold Wave occurrences, in event-days, each year over the period of record (19.0 years) is used to estimate the annualized frequency of Cold Waves in an area. Because a Cold Wave event can occur over several days or a single day, an event-day basis was used to estimate annualized frequency as this method better captu

ized frequency of Cold Waves in an area. Because a Cold Wave event can occur over several days or a single day, an event-day basis was used to estimate annualized frequency as this method better captures the variability in duration between occurrences. The annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level, and this Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations use the same intersection between Cold Wave event-days (or Cold Wave Date Expansion) polygons and Census block polygons that were used to calculate exposure. The count of distinct Cold Wave event-day polygons intersecting each Census block is recorded and used to calculate the annualized frequency of Cold Wave event-days as in Equation 36. Cold Wave Historic Loss Ratio The Cold Wave Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to a Cold Wave event-day, or the average rate of loss associated with the occurrence of a Cold Wave event-day. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Cold Wave hazard type. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS64 at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated. SHELDUS events from 1996 through 2023 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation. Four peril types are mapped to the hazard Cold Wave (see Table 30). These native records are expanded on an event-day basis (to a maximum of 31 event-days) and aggregated on a single-event-per-day basis (see

ril types are mapped to the hazard Cold Wave (see Table 30). These native records are expanded on an event-day basis (to a maximum of 31 event-days) and aggregated on a single-event-per-day basis (see Section 5.4.4 Historic Loss Ratio Calculation Methodology). 64 For Heat Wave loss information, SHELDUS compiles data from the monthly Storm Data publication produced by National Oceanic and Atmospheric Association’s National Centers for Environmental Information.

The Expected Annual Loss Score represents a community’s national percentile ranking relative to all communities at the same level. Building Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Cold Wave Expected Annual Loss for building by the total building value of the community. Population Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Cold Wave Expected Annual Loss for population by the total population of the community. Agriculture Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Cold Wave Expected Annual Loss for agriculture by the total agriculture value of the community. 8.8. Cold Wave Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of

s calculated by dividing the Cold Wave Expected Annual Loss for agriculture by the total agriculture value of the community. 8.8. Cold Wave Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  The Cold Wave hazard relies on Centers for Disease Control and Prevention data for fatality counts to estimate Population consequence type losses. These Compressed Mortality data are suppressed at the county level or at any geographic level where there are fewer than 10

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 8-22 reported fatalities. Additionally, mortality statistics are reported by the location of the decedent’s residence, not necessarily by the location where the fatality occurred.  There is currently no adequate injury information in SHELDUS to have an injury Historic Loss Ratio distinct from the fatality Historic Loss Ratio.  The ICD-10 Codes in the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention WONDER do not consider contributing causes of death based on the Cold hazard type, only underlying causes of death.  National Weather Service Warnings and Advisories are issued at the discretion of each individual National Weather Service Forecast Office, based on regionally determined thresholds. Though this accounts for varying cold-related capacities across the country (Alaska, for example, is more accustomed to lower temperatures than Florida), it results in an inconsistent record of dangerous cold events.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 9-1 9. Drought A Drought is a deficiency of precipitation over an extended period of time resulting in a water shortage. Drought hazard modeling uses datasets from the National Drought Mitigation Center and SHELDUS as the basis for calculating historical event impact areas and associated losses. The Drought hazard only calculates risk for the Agriculture consequence type. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 9.1. Drought Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Source: University of Nebraska-Lincoln National Drought Mitigation Center (NDMC), United States Drought Monitor66 The National Drought Mitigation Center provides shapefiles representing areas experiencing Drought on a weekly basis since 2000 (see Figure 30). Each Drought polygon is categorized by intensity from Abnormally Dry to Exceptional Drought. The Drought Monitor uses multiple indices and

encing Drought on a weekly basis since 2000 (see Figure 30). Each Drought polygon is categorized by intensity from Abnormally Dry to Exceptional Drought. The Drought Monitor uses multiple indices and indicators to classify Drought severity, and they rely on local condition reports from expert observers (see Table 32). Drought Intensity D1 Moderate Drought Drought Intensity D2 Severe Drought Drought Intensity D3 Extreme Drought Drought Intensity D4 Exceptional Drought 9.2. Drought Spatial Data Processing The drought shapefiles associated with each week from January 2000 through March 2025 were extracted and loaded into the processing database. The data initially consisted of 11,885 drought- week records. Only the most severe Drought events were analyzed, so only Drought Intensity categories D3 (Extreme Drought) and D4 (Exceptional Drought) were utilized. Drought-week polygons were then intersected with the Census tract polygons to calculate exposure and annualized frequency. 9.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Drought can occur almost anywhere und

gons were then intersected with the Census tract polygons to calculate exposure and annualized frequency. 9.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Drought can occur almost anywhere under the right conditions, so all counties were deemed possible for Drought occurrence. 9.3. Drought Non-Spatial Data Processing 9.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Drought in event-days is computed as the number of distinct Drought event-week polygons that intersect a Census tract multiplied by seven. This count uses the same Drought Census tract intersection table used to find exposure at the Census tract level and are used to compute annualized frequency at the Census tract level. A historical event-day count is also supplied at the county level as the number of distinct Drought event-week polygons that intersect the county multiplied by seven. 9.3.2. of distinct Drought event-week polygons that intersect the county multiplied by seven. 9.3.2. PERIOD OF RECORD The United States Drought Monitor data include Droughts from Jan. 01, 2000 to Mar. 31, 2025, so the period of record for which Drought data are utilized is 25.25 years. December 2025 9-2

National Risk Index Technical Documentation 9-3 9.4. Drought Exposure Only the Agriculture consequence type is considered for the Drought hazard. However, according to the United States Department of Agriculture Farm Service Agency Guidelines for the Livestock Indemnity Program, drought is not considered a primary cause of livestock death in the United States. 67 So, for the Drought hazard, only the crop portion of the Agriculture consequence type is considered in the exposure. To identify areas of exposure, the Drought-week polygons are intersected with the Census tract polygons within the processing database. The resulting table contains the Drought-week polygon’s unique identifier, Census tract number, the intersected area, and the area of intersection containing crops (see Table 33). All areas are square kilometers. Drought event-days can occur with a high frequency in areas but often result in no recorded loss to agriculture. SHELDUS does not record events in which no loss occurred, so a number of zero-loss event-days are inserted into the data to align the event-day count in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation to the historical event-day count experienced within the SHELDUS period of record (1996 through 2023). For Drought, the historical event-day count is extracted using the intersection between the Drought event-week polygons and the Census tract used to calculate annualized frequency and multiplying by 7 to convert weeks into days. An annual rate is calcula

ed using the intersection between the Drought event-week polygons and the Census tract used to calculate annualized frequency and multiplying by 7 to convert weeks into days. An annual rate is calculated as the event- day count divided by the period of record of 25.25 years, and this rate is multiplied by the SHELDUS period of record of 28 years to estimate a historical event-day count for the appropriate time range. If the number of loss-causing Drought event-day records from SHELDUS is less than the scaled event-day count for the county, then a number of zero-loss records equal to the difference are inserted into the Loss Ratio per Basis table with zero values for the consequence ratios. After the Loss Ratios per Basis are calculated for all Drought events, various sets of Loss Ratios per Basis are created for use in the Historic Loss Ratio Bayesian credibility process. Drought Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  There is often a disconnect between where a drought happens and where its impacts are felt, making direct economic losses a difficult component to measure. Drought risk is also geographically variable – some regions depend on snowpack for its water supply, while others are more dependent on liquid precipitatio

mic losses a difficult component to measure. Drought risk is also geographically variable – some regions depend on snowpack for its water supply, while others are more dependent on liquid precipitation. Additionally, mitigative efforts and legislative policies on water use can significantly alter the impacts of a drought occurrence. As such, modeling and measuring the impacts of Drought is an incredibly complex undertaking.  This only measures the direct impacts of Drought on crops; livestock is not considered because it is assumed that other mitigative activities would be implemented to protect livestock.  Time is a critical component for measuring Drought impacts. The timing and seasonality of a drought occurrence as well as individual crop viability impacts potential losses. Additionally, Drought is not a discretely occurring hazard and can last anywhere from weeks to years at a time. Information regarding flash droughts, which are rapid-onset drought conditions brought about by a

lly, Drought is not a discretely occurring hazard and can last anywhere from weeks to years at a time. Information regarding flash droughts, which are rapid-onset drought conditions brought about by a combination of low precipitation and elevated temperatures, high winds, and/or changes in radiation72 are also still limited. 72 https://www.drought.gov/what-is-drought/flash-drought

Earthquake An Earthquake is a shaking of the earth’s surface by energy waves emitted by slowly moving tectonic plates overcoming friction with one another underneath the earth’s surface. The Earthquake hazard is modeled by combining seismic frequency data from the U.S. Geological Survey to provide the hazard-susceptible area, and the Expected Annual Loss data provided by the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Hazus Earthquake Analysis. It is important to note that underlying hazard susceptibility data for Earthquake does not rely on observed historical events, but on probabilistic hazard data, meaning that the annualized frequency and historic loss ratio values are surrogate values and not used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. In addition, the Exposure and Expected Annual Loss values are sourced directly from the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Hazus Earthquake Analysis for both Census tract and county

s calculations. In addition, the Exposure and Expected Annual Loss values are sourced directly from the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Hazus Earthquake Analysis for both Census tract and county level values. The Earthquake hazard calculates risk for only the Building and Population consequence types. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 10.1. Then, Thiessen polygons (Voronoi diagram) were generated from the point data to represent areas closest to each point, creating a grid where the point values were applied to the grid cell. here the point values were applied to the grid cell. This grid provides the annualized frequency data for the United States and the territories of Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, Guam, Northern Mariana Islands, and American Samoa integrated with the necessary spatial attributes. Earthquake Expected Annual Loss Expected Annual Loss values are extracted from the Federal Emergency Management Agency Hazus Earthquake Analysis data at the Census tract and county levels. Exposure, annualized frequency, and Historic Loss Ratio are provided at the Census tract and county level as surrogate values but are not used to compute the Expected Annual Loss values. The Federal Emergency Management Agency Hazus Earthquake Analysis data estimate the Annualized Earthquake Losses, the estimated long-term value of earthquake losses to the general building stock in any single year in a specified geographic area, as well as an annualized population loss value. The Annualized Earthquake Loss is estimated using

of earthquake losses to the general building stock in any single year in a specified geographic area, as well as an annualized population loss value. The Annualized Earthquake Loss is estimated using the Riemann Sums Method. This method averages the annual losses associated with various return periods, averages the annual losses associated with each of the eight return periods, and then multiplies the resulting losses by their differential probability to develop one loss value for each Census tract. 74 The population loss estimation is based on the correlation between building damage and the number and severity of 74 FEMA. ssion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  Earthquake risk is based on probabilistic data for earthquake events and not on historical earthquake events. This differs from the way frequency is measured in other hazards, where the frequency measures hazard occurrence, regardless of whether an event results in damages.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 11-1 11. Hail Hail is a form of precipitation that occurs during thunderstorms when raindrops, in extremely cold areas of the atmosphere, freeze into balls of ice before falling towards the earth’s surface. The primary dataset available from the Storm Prediction Center includes all hail event records and basic latitude-longitude data which are interpolated into segments, then buffered to create hail impact areas for loss estimation. These are combined with calculated annualized frequency and SHELDUS-derived historic loss ratios to create the Expected Annual Loss. The Hail hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 11.1. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 11-2 Figure 40: Map of Hail Source Data Points 11.2. Hail Spatial Data Processing The source data includes fields for two sets of coordinates: a start and an end. This is mainly because the data share their format with the data for tornadoes. Most Hail events only have start coordinates (or the end coordinates match the start coordinates), so the points are projected from these coordinates. Any events outside of the period of record were filtered out. Additionally, smaller Hail size events were filtered out. Due to changes in National Weather Service standards for reportable Hail, events before 2010 were required to meet a Hail size threshold of 0.75-in, and those after 2010 must meet a 1.0-in size threshold. Anything below the threshold was not used in the analysis of Hail Expected Annual Loss. An 80-km buffer was created from the remaining points. The resulting Hail event polygons were used to estimate annualized frequency at

shold was not used in the analysis of Hail Expected Annual Loss. An 80-km buffer was created from the remaining points. The resulting Hail event polygons were used to estimate annualized frequency at the Census block level. The buffer is not an attempt to represent the area of impact by a Hail event, but rather an effort to estimate the area where Hail may have been present. Hail reporting can be influenced by urban bias, meaning that a Hail event in a populated area is more likely to be reported than if the same event had occurred in a rural area. Additionally, the position of the Hail event reported in the source data is not guaranteed to be the actual location of the occurrence but may be the location of a nearby weather station or reporting center. The use of the 80-km buffering allows the reported location to be spread across a broader area (see Figure 41).

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 11-3 Fi gure 41: Map of Buffered Hail Points 11.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Hail can occur almost anywhere under the right conditions, so all counties were deemed possible for Hail occurrence. 11.3. Hail Non-Spatial Data Processing 11.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Hail in events is initially computed as the number of distinct Hail event polygons that intersect a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid cell. Buffering the Hail points and using the fishnet grid to count historical Hail events serves to spatially spread the influence of past Hail events to nearby areas that may also be susceptible to Hail but have not experienced Hail as frequently. However, using these methods can overestimate Hail frequency. To adjust for this, a national scaling factor is calculated (see Equation 55). Hail Annualized Frequency The number of recorded Hail occurrences in events each year over the period of record (38.0 years) is used to estimate the annualized frequency of Hail events in an area. This annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level, and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations use the Hail event polygons created from the source data (as described in Section 11.2 Hail Spatial Data Processing), as well as their corresponding computed duration days from the pre-processing of the data. The Census block Hail event count computed using the scaled event counts of the fishnet grid cells intersecting the Census block is divided by the period of record to compute frequency as in Equation 57. Hail Historic Loss Ratio The Hail Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to a Hail event, or the average rate of loss associated with the occurrence of a Hail event. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Hail hazard type. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS76 at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated. SHELDUS events from 1996 through 2023 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation. One peril type is mapped to the hazard Hail (see Table 39). Native records of Hail storms that caused loss over more than one day (such as those that occurred overnight) have their loss assigned to the first day, and all records are aggregated on a single-event-per-day basis (see Section 5.4.4 Historic L

s over more than one day (such as those that occurred overnight) have their loss assigned to the first day, and all records are aggregated on a single-event-per-day basis (see Section 5.4.4 Historic Loss Rat io Calculation Methodology). Hail events can occur with a high frequency in areas, but often result in no recorded loss to buildings, population, or agriculture. SHELDUS does not record events in which no loss occurred, so a number of zero-loss events are inserted into the data to align the event count in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation to the historical event count experienced within the SHELDUS period of record (1996 through 2023). For Hail, the historical event count is extracted using an intersection between the Census blocks and the Hail event polygons for the years 1986-2023. An annual rate is calculated as the event count divided by the event polygon period of record of 38.0 years, and this rate is multiplied by the SHELDUS period of record of 28 years to estimate a historical event count for the appropriate time range. The Expected Annual Loss Score represents a community’s national percentile ranking relative to all communities at the same level. Building Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Hail Expected Annual Loss for buildings by the total building value of the community. Population Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Hail Expected Annual Loss for population by the total population of the community. Agriculture Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Hail Expected Annual Loss for agriculture by the total agriculture value of the community. 11.8. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 12-1 12. Heat Wave A Heat Wave is a period of abnormally and uncomfortably hot and unusually humid weather typically lasting two or more days with temperatures outside the historical averages for a given area. The temperatures classified as Heat Wave are dependent on the location and defined by the local National Weather Service weather forecast office. Similar to Cold Wave, the Heat Wave hazard modeling uses Mesonet data that are derived from the National Weather Service’s warnings and advisories to provide impact areas for this hazard. SHELDUS data were used for historical losses for Heat Wave events and to generate the Historic Loss Ratios, with data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s Compressed Mortality Database used to supplement the SHELDUS mortality data. These are overlaid with the exposure values provided in Hazus 6.0 for Building, Population, and Agriculture for the final Expected Annual Loss calcul

ase used to supplement the SHELDUS mortality data. These are overlaid with the exposure values provided in Hazus 6.0 for Building, Population, and Agriculture for the final Expected Annual Loss calculations. The Heat Wave hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 12.1. Heat Wave Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Compiling Source: Iowa State University, Iowa Environmental Mesonet77 The National Weather Service is continuously issuing weather alerts based on current weather conditions. 78 Each alert is coded by type and significance and can conceptually serve as documentation of the potential for weather event activities in a specific area. Archived National Weather Service alerts are aggregated, continuously updated, and made available for download in shapefile format by Iowa State University's Iowa Environmental M

s in a specific area. Archived National Weather Service alerts are aggregated, continuously updated, and made available for download in shapefile format by Iowa State University's Iowa Environmental Mesonet. Data include geometry for each alert's issued area and attributes related to each alert’s severity and phenomena type. Weather alerts are also timestamped with the time of issuance and the time of expiration. A table describing this dataset’s attributes can be found in Appendix C – Mesonet-National Weather Service Weather Event Attribute Description. The geometry shape for each alert record represents the geographic area for which the National Weather Service alert applied. However, the Mesonet shapes are simplified versions of the more detailed National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shape originally associated with the alert record. Because the Mesonet tabular data still retains the reference ID for the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone, it is used to relate to

Zone shape originally associated with the alert record. Because the Mesonet tabular data still retains the reference ID for the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone, it is used to relate to the zone associated with each alert record. The National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones can be downloaded in shapefile format79 and represent the codified areas for which weather alerts are issued by National Weather Service. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 12-2 Public Forecast Zones shape definitions are predominantly derived from county boundaries. While the Public Forecast Zone boundaries are more refined than those substituted into the Mesonet data, they are not at the same resolution as the current county boundaries derived from Census blocks. Utilizing the Public Forecast Zone shapefile in conjunction with the Public Forecast Zone – County Correlation file, 80 a determination was made as to which Public Forecast Zones have single-county coverage and which are either sub-county zones or made of portions of multiple counties. For perspective, the following approximate distributions of forecast zone composition were found:  70% of the zones are single-county coverage.  20% are cases where a single county is subdivided into multiple zones.  10% are zones that breach parts of multiple contiguous counties. For thos e Forecast Zones covering a single county, the United States Cen

es where a single county is subdivided into multiple zones.  10% are zones that breach parts of multiple contiguous counties. For thos e Forecast Zones covering a single county, the United States Census Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing 2021 county boundaries (Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing 2024 boundaries for Connecticut) are substituted. Another aspect of the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones is that they can and have changed over time. In the Mesonet data (2005 through 2024), there are many distinct Forecast Zones referenced that do not exist in the current National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shapefile. This occurs when a National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone has been modified in shape, renamed, and/or “retired” from use. The National Weather Service maintains a downloadable Change History log of the various changes in Forecast Zone areas since 1997. This text file does not contain the pre- nor

/or “retired” from use. The National Weather Service maintains a downloadable Change History log of the various changes in Forecast Zone areas since 1997. This text file does not contain the pre- nor post-shape of the altered forecast zone. Archived versions of these changes are likely available via contact with National Weather Service, but the effort to match the National Weather Service issued alert record to the version-controlled shape representation of the forecast zone at the time of alert issue is beyond the scope of the processing effort, though a Mesonet representative was contacted to see if Forecast Zone shapes associated with each year of alert data had been archived. Unfortunately, no such archival information is available. For cases where the more refined National Weather Service Forecast Zone shape is unavailable, the simplified Mesonet boundary version shape is used. Heat Wave Spatial Processing Heat Wave exposure and annualized frequency values are determined by taking the spatial intersection of the National Weather Service Heat Wave event polygons and Census block geometries. Before this analysis could occur, the raw National Weather Service and Census data were prepared using the procedures outlined below. Heat Wave weather event alerts were first extracted from the dataset based on the Valid Time Extent Code significance code (SIG field) and the phenomena code (PHENOM or TYPE field) values. Only Warning alerts (SIG = ‘W’) and Advisory alerts (SIG = ‘Y’) of the Phenomena type “Excessive Heat” (EH) or “Heat” (HT) were considered Heat Wave occurrences (see Table 41); Watch alerts (SIG = ‘A’) are excluded from this analysis. To remove unintended error in spatial results due to the use

Heat” (EH) or “Heat” (HT) were considered Heat Wave occurrences (see Table 41); Watch alerts (SIG = ‘A’) are excluded from this analysis. To remove unintended error in spatial results due to the use of the simplified event area shapes contained in the Mesonet data, event areas with a higher resolution version were substituted. This substitution used the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shape associated with the alert record or, in cases where the forecast zone is for a single county, a better resolution version of the county boundary area.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation Table 41: Original Mesonet Heat Wave Records WFO ISSUED EXPIRED PHENOM SIG NWS_UGC AREA_KM2 PSR June 04, 2017 6:00 PM June 08, 2017 3:00 AM EH W AZZ554 583.5392 MAF June 17, 2017 5:00 PM June 18, 2017 2:00 AM EH W TXZ045 3894.574 VEF June 17, 2017 6:00 PM June 27, 2017 4:00 AM EH W NVZ017 7555.405 Heat Wave occurrences are measured in event-days as this more accurately represents the variability of Heat Wave event duration. To capture this, each native alert record with a duration greater than a single day was replaced with multiple records, one for each day of the original record’s duration. If a Heat Wave event’s duration on any given day is less than 6 hours, then the event was assigned to the day having the greatest duration of the event. This handled cases where the event occurs in the late evening but endures for a greater length of time on the next calendar day than on the day the alert was issued. For cases where the event duration is

s handled cases where the event occurs in the late evening but endures for a greater length of time on the next calendar day than on the day the alert was issued. For cases where the event duration is longer, the following business logic was used: If a weather event’s duration is greater than 6 hours, assign the event to all days on which 6 or more hours occur. For example, if a 14-hour weather event was issued for 2 AM until 6 PM on July 1, then the event would be assigned to July 1. If the alert was issued from 11 PM on July 1 to 1 PM on July 2, then the event would be assigned to only July 2. If the alert was issued from 6 PM on July 1 to 9 AM on July 2, then the event would be assigned to both July 1 and July 2. To illustrate this concept, the Heat Wave events in Table 42 are expanded to create the Heat Wave event-day records in Table 43. to create the Heat Wave event-day records in Table 43. Additionally, there are some data quality issues with the Mesonet data. For example, some warnings have an expiration date that is prior to the issue date. In these cases, a single record was used and assigned the issue date. to combine all Heat Wave event areas occurring on the same day (Year, Month, Day specific) into one representative event shape was performed. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Heat Waves can occur almost anywhere in the United States as the definition of a Heat Wave is locally defined by the area’s weather forecast office. For example, a forecast office in Texas may define a Heat Wave differently than a forecast office in New York. Therefore, all counties were deemed possible for Heat Wave occurrence.81 81 On June 2, 2025, the Fairbanks and Juneau, AK Weather Forecast Offices replaced the use of Special Weather Statements with Heat Advisories (https://www.weather.gov/media/notification/pdf_2025/scn25- 39_heat_advisory

, the Fairbanks and Juneau, AK Weather Forecast Offices replaced the use of Special Weather Statements with Heat Advisories (https://www.weather.gov/media/notification/pdf_2025/scn25- 39_heat_advisory_public_notice_AR.pdf ). Prior to this date, Alaska had no Heat Advisory system in place and was therefore excluded from this analysis due to having insufficient data. Alaska’s first ever Heat Advisory was issued on June 15, 2025.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 12-6 12.3. Heat Wave Non-Spatial Data Processing 12.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Heat Wave, in event-days, is computed as the area-weighted sum of distinct Heat Wave event-day polygons that intersect a Census block. This count uses the same Heat Wave expansion Census block intersection table used to calculate exposure and is used to compute annualized frequency at the Census block level. Historical event-day counts are also supplied at the Census tract and county levels as the number of distinct Heat Wave event-day polygons that intersect the Census tract and county, respectively. 12.3.2. PERIOD OF RECORD The National Weather Service’s system of recording watch, warnings, and advisories (WWA) in the 1990s and early 2000s was too difficult for automated processing. In 2005 the Valid Time Extent Code system was implemented, which allowed for the easy automated parsing of alert data. Therefore

n the 1990s and early 2000s was too difficult for automated processing. In 2005 the Valid Time Extent Code system was implemented, which allowed for the easy automated parsing of alert data. Therefore, National Weather Service weather events data were downloaded for 2005 through 2024. The date range is Nov. 12, 2005 to Nov. 12, 2024, so the period of record for which Heat Wave data are utilized is 19.0 years. 12.4. Heat Wave Exposure To identify areas of exposure, the Heat Wave event-day polygons (also referred to as Heat Wave Date Expansions to acknowledge the spatiotemporal processing described in Section 12.2 Heat Wave Spatial Processing) are intersected with the Census block polygons within the processing database. The resulting table contains the Heat Wave event-day’s unique identifier, Census block number, and the intersected area in square kilometers (see Table 44). National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 12-8 It should be noted that, in order for a Heat Wave event-day polygon’s intersection with a Census block to be included, the area of the intersection must cover at least 5% of the Census block. This is a spatial modeling technique to correct for the small intersect “slivers” generated by differing versions of county boundary geometry being used. Because the exposure model uses a conservative-case concentration of exposure and a developed area density value, it is possible to mathematically generate an exposure value that is greater than the total value of the Census block. The Hazus-recorded population and building value and the Census of Agriculture-reported crop and livestock value for the Census block are considered ceilings on exposure. For example, if the calculated exposed building value exceeds the Hazus-recorded building value, then the Hazus-recorded building value is used as the building exposure value for the Censu

n exposure. For example, if the calculated exposed building value exceeds the Hazus-recorded building value, then the Hazus-recorded building value is used as the building exposure value for the Census block. 12.4.1. Heat Wave Annualized Frequency The number of recorded Heat Wave occurrences, in event-days, each year over the period of record (19.0 years) is used to estimate the annualized frequency of Heat Waves in an area. Because a Heat Wave event can last over several days or a single day, an event-day basis was used to estimate annualized frequency as this method better captures the variability in duration between eve

ecause a Heat Wave event can last over several days or a single day, an event-day basis was used to estimate annualized frequency as this method better captures the variability in duration between events. The annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level, and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 12-10 Annualized frequency calculations use the same intersection between Heat Wave event-days (or Heat Wave Date Expansion) polygons and Census block polygons that were used to calculate exposure. The count of distinct Heat Wave event-day polygons intersecting each Census block is recorded and used to calculate the annualized frequency of Heat Wave event-days as in Equation 64. Heat Wave Historic Loss Ratio The Heat Wave Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to a Heat Wave event-day, or the average rate of loss associated with the occurrence of a Heat Wave event-day. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Heat Wave hazard type. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS83 at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated. SHELDUS events from 1996 through 2023 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation. Two peril types are mapped to the hazard Heat Wave (see Table 45). ble 45). These native records are expanded on an event-day basis (to a maximum of 31 event-days) and aggregated on a single-event-per-day basis (see Section 5.4.4 Historic Loss Ratio Calculation Methodology). Heat Wave event-days can occur with a high frequency in areas but often result in no recorded loss to building value, population, or agriculture value. SHELDUS does not record events in which no loss occurred, so zero-loss event-days are inserted into the data to align the event count in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation to the historical event count experienced within the SHELDUS period of record (1996 through 2023). For Heat Wave, the historical event-day count is extracted using the intersection between the Heat Wave event-day polygons from the years 2005-2024 and the Census block polygons used to calculate exposure and annualized fre

orical event-day count is extracted using the intersection between the Heat Wave event-day polygons from the years 2005-2024 and the Census block polygons used to calculate exposure and annualized frequency (see Table 44). An annual rate is calculated as the event-day count divided by the period of record of 19.0 years, and this rate is multiplied by the SHELDUS period of record of 28 years to estimate a historical event-day count for the appropriate time range. If the number of Heat Wave event-day records from SHELDUS is less than the scaled event-day count for the county, then a number of zero-loss records equal to the difference are inserted into the Loss Ratio per Basis table with zero values for the consequence ratios. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 12-21 With the Heat Wave total Expected Annual Loss Value computed for each Census tract and county, the companion Expected Annual Loss Score is computed (see Section 3.2 Values, Scores and Ratings). The Expected Annual Loss score represents a community’s national percentile ranking relative to all communities at the same level. Building Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Heat Wave Expected Annual Loss for building by the total building value of the community. Population Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Heat Wave Expected Annual Loss for population by the total population of the community. Agriculture Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Heat Wave Expected Annual Loss for agriculture by the total agriculture value of the community. 12.8. r agriculture by the total agriculture value of the community. 12.8. Heat Wave Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  National Weather Service Warnings and Advisories are issued at the discretion of each individual National Weather Service Forecast Office, based on regionally determined thresholds. Though this accounts for varying heat-related capacities across the country (Florida, for example, is more accustomed to higher temperatures than Alaska), it results in an inconsistent record of dangerous heat events.  The Heat Wave hazard relies on Centers for Disease Control and Prevention data for fatality counts to estimate Population consequence type losses. These Compressed Mortality data are suppresse

ents.  The Heat Wave hazard relies on Centers for Disease Control and Prevention data for fatality counts to estimate Population consequence type losses. These Compressed Mortality data are suppressed at the county level or at any geographic level where there are fewer than 10 reported fatalities. Additionally, mortality statistics are reported by the location of the decedent’s residence, and not necessarily by the location where the fatality occurred.  There is currently no adequate injury information in SHELDUS to have an injury Historic Loss Ratio distinct from the fatality Historic Loss Ratio.  The ICD-10 Codes in CDC WONDER do not consider contributing causes of death based on the Heat hazard type, only underlying causes of death.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation 13- 1 13. Hurricane A Hurricane is a tropical cyclone or localized, low-pressure weather system that has organized thunderstorms but no front (a boundary separating two air masses of different densities) and maximum sustained winds of at least 74 miles per hour (mph). The Hurricane data also includes tropical storms for which wind speeds range from 39 to 74 mph. The model for the Hurricane hazard is intended to reflect only the wind-related risks of hurricane events, not the flooding risks as those are incorporated into the Inland Flooding and Coastal Flooding hazards. As such, the methodology and data used to model hurricane frequency, historical occurrences, and exposure areas are based on hurricane storm tracks and windfields. Using data from the National Hurricane Center for storm tracks and windfield extents and historical loss data from SHELDUS, this methodology identifies Census blocks where hurricane wind impacts are possible. The hur

the National Hurricane Center for storm tracks and windfield extents and historical loss data from SHELDUS, this methodology identifies Census blocks where hurricane wind impacts are possible. The hurricane hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 13.1. source of information on Atlantic tropical and subtropical cyclones. 86 It contains a series of storm observation records at six-hour intervals with location, maximum wind speed, central pressure, and (beginning in 2004) cyclone size. Historical Occurrence Source: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, Historical Hurricane Tracks 88 HURDAT2 does not include data on storm tracks in the Western or Southern Pacific Ocean, effectively excluding most storms that have historically impacted the Pacific territories. Data from the Historical Hurricane Tracks were therefore used to establish annualized frequency estimates for Guam, American Samoa, and Northern Mariana Islands. The Historical Hurricane Tracks is a free web-based tool developed by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s Office for Coastal Management with the National Hurricane Center and National Centers for Environmental Information. The tool contains global hurricane data for over 13,000 historica

Administration’s Office for Coastal Management with the National Hurricane Center and National Centers for Environmental Information. The tool contains global hurricane data for over 13,000 historical storm tracks dating as far back as 1851, with detailed reports for United States tropical cyclones since 1958. Storm track information displayed in the web-based app is sourced from HURDAT2 and National Centers for Environmental Information’s International Best Track Archive for Climate Stewardship datasets. 13.2. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE To distinguish between areas where no Hurricane events have occurred and those where such events are not deemed possible, a control table was generated to designate which counties have some probability of Hurricane occurrence. The Hurricane event path polygons processed to represent historical storms as described in Section 13.2 Hurrica ne Spatial Processing were buffered to an additional 100 miles, and for any Census blocks that intersected at least one buffered Hurricane event path polygon, their counties were included with some probability of event occurrence. Additionally, a subset of inland counties near the Atlantic basin that had sustained historical economic loss to Hurricanes according to SHELDUS was also included (see Figure 57). This count uses the same Hurricane Census block intersection table used to calculate exposure. PERIOD OF RECORD The hurricane track datasets are organized by ocean basins: Atlantic and Pacific. The storms in the Atlantic datasets date from Jan. 01, 1851 to Sept. 30, 2024 (173.86 years), while those in the Pacific date from Jan. 01, 1949 to Nov. 30, 2024 (76.0 years). 13.4. Hurricane Exposure To identify areas of exposure, the Hurricane event path polygons are intersected with the Census block polygons within the processing database. The resulting table contains the storm’s unique identifier, Census block number, and the intersected area in square kilometers (see Table 50). Hurricane Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the estimated number of recorded Hurricane occurrences, in events, each year for a specific area. This annualized frequency is utilized at the Census block level, and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected An

f recorded Hurricane occurrences, in events, each year for a specific area. This annualized frequency is utilized at the Census block level, and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations are determined by intersecting the same buffered Hurricane event path polygons that are used to calculate exposure with a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid. The count of distinct Hurricane event path polygons intersecting each grid cell is recorded, and each Census block inherits this fishnet-aggregated count from the grid cell that encompasses it. If the Census block intersects multiple fishnet grid cells, an area-weighted average count is calculated (see Appendix D – Fishnet Occurrence Count). The Hurricane event count (determined from the fishnet-aggregated count) is then divided by the period of record (depending on the ocean basin of the location) as in Equation 74.
