exposure (see Table 50). Unlike the count used for annualized frequency, this count is simply the number of distinct Hurricane event path polygons that have intersected Census blocks in the county. An annual rate is calculated as the event count divided by the period of record of 173.86 years for the Atlantic basin or 76.0 years for the Pacific basin. This rate is multiplied by the SHELDUS period of record of 28 years to estimate a historical event-day count for the appropriate time range. Because SHELDUS does not include any Hurricane loss records for the Pacific territories, the highest Historic Loss Ratio from the Gulf Coast was applied. If the number of loss-causing Hurricane event records from SHELDUS is less than the scaled event count for the county, a number of zero-loss records equal to the difference are inserted into the Loss Ratio per Basis table with zero values for the consequence ratios. After the Loss Ratios per Basis are calculated for each county, county-level mean l

qual to the difference are inserted into the Loss Ratio per Basis table with zero values for the consequence ratios. After the Loss Ratios per Basis are calculated for each county, county-level mean loss ratios and event counts are computed and used as inputs into a frequency weighted Generalized Linear Model with a logit link function. The Generalized Linear Model generates Historic Loss Ratio estimates for each county, conditional on their distance from the Gulf/Atlantic coast, maximum sustained probabilistic wind speeds from the Hazus Hurricane inventory, National Center for Health Statistics Urban-Rural Classification, and Hurricane Historic Loss Ratio region definition. Hurricane Methodology Discussion This section brief

Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Hurricane Expected Annual Loss value for agriculture by the total agriculture value of the community. 13.8. Hurricane Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  The Hurricane hazard endeavors to reflect only the wind-related risks, not the associated inland flooding or coastal flooding risks. This is partially to avoid over-estimating risk, since both water-related hazards are already considered on their own. However, due to the inexact differentiation of water vs. wind-caused damages for historical hurricane events, the data

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 13-21 development team recognizes that the underlying data may combine losses from different hurricane impacts. Therefore, the hazard cannot at this time be accurately labeled as “hurricane wind,” though it is largely focused only on hurricane wind.  Alaska was designated as “Hazard Not Possible” for hurricane wind. Despite a single historical event impacting the area, the data development team determined that tropical storm impacts at the current time are insufficient to allow for comparison to the rest of the country.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 14-1 14. Ice Storm An Ice Storm is a freezing rain situation (rain that freezes on surface contact) with significant ice accumulations of 0.25 inches or greater. The Ice Storm hazard is based on a static dataset that has not been updated since 2014. While lacking data updates on the geographic extent of ice storm occurrences in the past ten years, the data were still sufficient for hazard susceptibility determinations. The historical loss data in SHELDUS provided sufficient loss records for creating Historic Loss Ratios and valid Expected Annual Loss values. The Ice Storm hazard only calculates risk for the Building and Population consequence types. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 14.1. Ice Storm Spatial Source Data Historical Event Source: USACE Cold Regions Research and Engineering Laboratory, Damaging Ice Storm GIS94 The Cold R egions Research a

a Characteristics Comparison. 14.1. Ice Storm Spatial Source Data Historical Event Source: USACE Cold Regions Research and Engineering Laboratory, Damaging Ice Storm GIS94 The Cold R egions Research and Engineering Laboratory Damaging Ice Storm GIS database includes footprint polygons representing the area where ice-sensitive structures (i.e., overhead power, phone and cable television lines, communication towers, and trees) were damaged by freezing rain storms in a subset of storms between 1946 and the spring of 2014, with modeled ice thicknesses designated as significant based on an established 50-year mean recurrence interval (see Figure 64). Start and end dates for Ice Storm occurrences are also included in the data. Ice Storms that cause only slippery roads are not included. This data source is not complete for all years in the period of record, as many weather stations did not begin storing electronic records until the early 1970s. 94 CRREL, USACE. (2014). Damaging Ice Storm GIS

source is not complete for all years in the period of record, as many weather stations did not begin storing electronic records until the early 1970s. 94 CRREL, USACE. (2014). Damaging Ice Storm GIS [online dataset]. Retrieved from https://www.erdc.usace.army.mil/Media/Fact-Sheets/Fact-Sheet-Article-View/Article/490684/damaging-ice-storm-gis/.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 14-2 Fi gure 64: Map of Sample Damaging Ice Storm Polygons 14.2. Ice Storm Spatial Data Processing Because the source data provides Ice Storm footprint polygons that work well, no spatial pre- processing was necessary beyond projecting the data to the North America Albers Equal Area Conic projection. However, some inaccuracies could be found in the storm event start and end dates, such as end dates that preceded their start dates or exceptionally long storms that were deemed suspect. Once Ice Storm durations were calculated, any negative or zero-day durations were set to 1, while any storms longer than 30 days were capped at 30. These durations were used to estimate exposure and annualized frequency. 14.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE To distinguish between areas with no Ice Storm occurrences and those where such occurrences are not deemed possible, a control table was generated to designate which counties

LITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE To distinguish between areas with no Ice Storm occurrences and those where such occurrences are not deemed possible, a control table was generated to designate which counties have some probability of Ice Storm occurrence. This was initially determined by selecting only counties that intersected a past Ice Storm footprint polygon. However, this selection was widened to include all counties in states that intersected a past Ice Storm footprint polygon, except Florida and Hawai‘i. Counties in Florida that intersected past Ice Storm footprint polygons were included as possible; however, the southern parts of the state that had not experienced an Ice Storm were not included. Any county that had sustained economic loss due to an Ice Storm as reported in SHELDUS was also included as one in which Ice Storm occurrence is possible. (See Figure 65).

HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Ice Storm, in event-days, is computed as the number of distinct Ice Storm event polygons that intersect a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid cell multiplied by the number of duration days associated with each Ice Storm occurrence (see Equation 79). PERIOD OF RECORD To capture the largest extent of credible data, records from Dec. 31, 1946 to Feb. 12, 2014 are analyzed. Ice Storm Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the estimated number of recorded Ice Storm occurrences, in event-days, each year for a specific area. This annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level, and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations use the Ice Storm footprint polygons from the source data as well as their corresponding computed duration days from the pre-processing of the data. The footprint polygons are intersected with a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid. The sum of Ice Storm event-days for the polygons intersecting each grid cell is recorded, and the Census block inherits t

data. The footprint polygons are intersected with a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid. The sum of Ice Storm event-days for the polygons intersecting each grid cell is recorded, and the Census block inherits this aggregated event- day count from the grid cell that encompasses it (see Equation 79). If the Census block intersects multiple fishnet grid cells, an area-weighted average count is calculated (see Appendix D – Fishnet Occurrence Count). Using this count, the Census block annualized frequency is calculated as in Equation 82. Ice Storm Historic Loss Ratio The Ice Storm Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to an Ice Storm event-day, or the average rate of loss associated with the occurrence of an Ice Storm event-day. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Ice Storm hazard type. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS95 at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated. SHELDUS events from 1996 through 2023 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation. One peril type is mapped to the hazard Ice Storm (see Table 53). peril type is mapped to the hazard Ice Storm (see Table 53). These native records are expanded on an event-day basis (to a maximum of 31 event-days) and aggregated on a single-event-per-day basis (see Section 5.4.4 Historic Loss Ratio Calculation Methodology). Ice Storm event-days may occur in areas without resulting in recorded loss to buildings or population. SHELDUS does not record events in which no loss occurred, so a number of zero-loss event-day records are inserted into the loss data to align the event count in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation to the historical event count experienced within the SHELDUS period of record (1996 through 2023). For Ice Storm, the historical event-day count is extracted using an intersection between the Ice Storm event-day polygons and the Census blocks. Unlike the count used for annualized frequency, this count is simply the summed duration days of distinct Ice Storm polygons that have intersected Census blocks in the county. An annual rate is calculated as the event

used for annualized frequency, this count is simply the summed duration days of distinct Ice Storm polygons that have intersected Census blocks in the county. An annual rate is calculated as the event-day count divided by the period of record of 67.16 years, and this rate is multiplied by the SHELDUS period of record of 28 years to estimate a historical event-day count for the appropriate time range. If the number of loss-causing Ice Storm event-day records from SHELDUS is less than the scaled event-day count for the county, then a number of zero-loss records equal to the difference are inserted into the Loss Ratio per Basis table with zero values for the consequence ratios. In an effort to correct for urban bias, a ceiling is applied to the Bayesian-adjusted population Historic Loss Ratio for Ice Storm. This is calculated as the average number of people (excluding zero population loss events) impacted by past Ice Storms per county divided by the county population. This affects a few highly populated counties where the Bayesian influence of injuries and fatalities in less populated surrounding counties may overinflate the Historic Loss Ratio of urban counties. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 14-16 the losses have been incorporated into their respective hazards, but that these may differ from users’ conceptualizations or expectations.  The underlying data for Ice Storm occurrences, sourced from the United States Army Corps of Engineers’ Cold Regions Research and Engineering Laboratory have not been updated since 2014. Any changes to trends in frequency and geographic extents of ice storm occurrences from 2014 through present day would therefore not be captured in loss estimations.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 15-1 15. Inland Flooding Inland Flooding occurs when streams and rivers exceed the capacity of their natural or constructed channels to accommodate water flow and water overflows the banks, spilling into adjacent low-lying, dry land (fluvial flooding), or when excessive rainfall overwhelms drainage systems leading to surface water accumulation (pluvial flooding). Inland flooding can occur anywhere it rains, even if there are no nearby waterbodies. The Inland Flooding hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. Methods for the building consequence type received significant updates due to the incorporation of new source data. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 15.1. Inland Flooding Spatial Source Data Susceptible Area Source: The Federal Emergency Management Agency, Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment The

e Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 15.1. Inland Flooding Spatial Source Data Susceptible Area Source: The Federal Emergency Management Agency, Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment The Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment contains Hazus dasymetric developed area polygon features. Each feature has an attribute value that describes an average annual loss rate due to flood hazard associated with a one-story single-family home with a slab-on-grade foundation. The model and framework are built on years of investment in flood hazard information and analysis through the National Flood Insurance Program and Risk Mapping, Assessment, and Planning program. The Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment depicts inland flood losses derived from geographic factors such as elevation, distance to rivers, and relative elevation from a river, combining catastrophe modeling, National Flood Insurance Program historical data, and actuarial pricing approach. This information is used to inform potential an

, and relative elevation from a river, combining catastrophe modeling, National Flood Insurance Program historical data, and actuarial pricing approach. This information is used to inform potential annualized loss rates for different building types at the dasymetric Census block level. Susceptible Area Source: The Federal Emergency Management Agency, National Flood Insurance Program, National Flood Hazard Layer96 The National Flood Hazard Layer contains several layers depicting flood information, including levee locations, Flood Insurance Rate Map boundaries, and floodplain polygons. The polygons for the 1% annual chance floodplain were downloaded in shapefile format (see Figure 70) for use in the calculation of Inland Flooding exposure. 96 National Flood Insurance Program, FEMA. (2025). NFHL [online dataset]. Retrieved from https://www.fema.gov/national- flood-hazard-layer-nfhl.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 15-2 Fi gure 70: Map of 1% Annual Chance Floodplain Susceptible Area Source: CoreLogic SFHA Layer The CoreLogic digitized floodplain boundaries supplement the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s official digital National Flood Hazard Layer data in areas where only paper Flood Insurance Rate Maps exist. These boundaries have been compiled by CoreLogic through the digitization of existing paper flood maps and the use of legacy paper Federal Emergency Management Agency products. The Federal Emergency Management Agency has licensed this data from CoreLogic to supplement its National Flood Hazard Layer data while the Federal Emergency Management Agency engages with communities where digital data-coverage gaps exist in the Federal Emergency Management Agency National Flood Hazard Layer. Historical Event Source: National Centers for Environmental Information, Storm Events Database97 The National Centers for Environmental Information

ency Management Agency National Flood Hazard Layer. Historical Event Source: National Centers for Environmental Information, Storm Events Database97 The National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database contains records of the occurrence of storms and other significant weather phenomena, including flooding events, since January 1950. Each flooding event record includes the affected counties, the dates of the event occurrence, and any reported loss. These records are used to calculate the annualized frequency for Inland Flooding. 97 National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI). (2020). Storm Events Database, Version 3.1. [online database]. Retrieved from https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/stormevents/.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 15-3 15.2. Inland Flooding Spatial Data Processing The flood hazard areas in which the Flood Zone Category begins with “A” were extracted from the CoreLogic data and the National Flood Hazard Layer data. This selection criteria extracted the 1% annual chance flood hazard areas associated with possible Inland Flooding, as well as coastal hazard areas that experienced shallow flow or ponding with water depths of 1 to 3 feet (“AH”). These two selections from the source data were then combined to form a single layer of polygons for the 1% annual chance Inland Flood hazard type. The resulting layer was used in annualized frequency calculations as well as the calculation of exposure for population and agriculture. For the building consequence type, the Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment provided baseline annualized loss rates at the Census block level and provided the foundation for calculating Expected Annual Loss for the building cons

ence type, the Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment provided baseline annualized loss rates at the Census block level and provided the foundation for calculating Expected Annual Loss for the building consequence type. These baseline loss rates describe the proportion of a Census block’s building value that would be lost due to flood hazard in a typical year if its entire building stock were made up of one-story single-family home with a slab-on-grade foundation. To account for differences in physical flood susceptibility across building types, baseline loss rates were adjusted for each building category defined in the Hazus Flood model based upon their relative damage function values for the flood depth of 3 feet before being applied directly to their respective building values. 15.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE On examining the economic loss records in SHELDUS, it was found that almost every county in the United States had sustained some form of loss due to Inland

INATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE On examining the economic loss records in SHELDUS, it was found that almost every county in the United States had sustained some form of loss due to Inland Flooding occurrences, so all counties were deemed possible for Inland Flooding occurrence. 15.3. Inland Flooding Non-Spatial Data Processing 15.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Inland Flooding, in event-days, is computed as the number of days in which Inland Flooding events (defined as having an Event Type of Flash Flood, Flood, Hail Flooding, Lakeshore Flood, Thunderstorm Winds/Flood, or Thunderstorm Winds/Flash Flood) were recorded in the National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database within the county from January 1996 to December 2023. Multiple event records that occur on the same day in the same county are counted as a single Inland Flooding event as these recorded events are likely due to the same cause (heavy rain, for example

3. Multiple event records that occur on the same day in the same county are counted as a single Inland Flooding event as these recorded events are likely due to the same cause (heavy rain, for example) but occur in distinct parts of the county. This count is only performed for counties that intersect the 1% annual chance inland floodplain. Historical event-day counts are also supplied at the Census tract level. These values are inherited from the parent county as the exact location of the occurrence within the county cannot always be determined from the National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database record.

Inland Flooding Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the number of Inland Flooding occurrences, in event- days, each year over the period of record (27 years). Annualized frequency is initially calculated at the county level. ected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Inland Flooding Expected Annual Loss for agriculture by the total agriculture value of the community. 15.8. Inland Flooding Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  The Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment loss rates used to estimate inland flooding risks are based on an assumed building type of a slab-on-grade, single-family home, with a replacement cost value of $250,000. This loss rate is useful as a figure for comparative risk

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 15-19 that exists across the nation, but the rate itself is unable to differentiate between different building types.  Loss calculations use generalized damage curves that correspond to certain types of buildings and may not accurately reflect the variety of building types in a given area. There is the potential for underestimation or overestimation of losses due to reliance on generalized damage functions rather than specific empirical data.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation 16- 1 16. Landslide A Landslide is the movement of a mass of rock, debris, or earth down a slope. The Landslide hazard incorporates the 2024 U.S. Geological Survey National Landslide Susceptibility Layer and the 2025 U.S. Geological Survey United States county-level landslide frequency data to determine Expected Annual Loss values for the Continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i. For landslide risk in Puerto Rico, the 2024 U.S. Geological Survey National Landslide Susceptibility Layer was used for susceptibility, but an independent method developed in partnership with subject matter experts was needed for landslide frequency. Puerto Rico landslide frequency was calculated using an inventory of Hurricane Maria-induced landslide locations to estimate landslide distribution and a post-1950’s inventory of landslide inducing events to calculate an annualized frequency. The Landslide hazard only calculates risk for Building and Population con

estimate landslide distribution and a post-1950’s inventory of landslide inducing events to calculate an annualized frequency. The Landslide hazard only calculates risk for Building and Population consequence types; it does not include Agriculture consequences. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 16-3 across the United States (Version 3, 2025 Update) to train a statistical model. The statistical model estimated county-level landslide frequencies as a function of standardized landslide susceptible area, standardized probability of potentially landslide triggering earthquakes, and ecoregion. For this model, landslide susceptible area was calculated using the 10-meter U.S. Geological Survey National Landslide Susceptibility Model. The average probability of an earthquake with a Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI) greater than or equal to VI in 100 years from the 2023 United States National Seismic Hazard Model was used to indicate potentially landslide triggering earthquake probability. Level I ecoregion served as a proxy for other environmental factors that may influence landslide frequency but are not explicitly modeled, for example, climate, land cover, and geology. 16.2. er environmental factors that may influence landslide frequency but are not explicitly modeled, for example, climate, land cover, and geology. 16.2. Landslide Spatial Data Processing 16.2.1. EXPOSURE PROCESSING Landslide exposure was determined by taking the intersection of the U.S. Geological Survey- identified landslide susceptible areas with each Census block in the United States and Puerto Rico. To do so, the raster formatted data were first converted to a vector format. Converting the raster dataset to vector format greatly improves the processing speed and repeatability of resource- intensive intersection functions performed within the processing database. The National Landslide Susceptibility Model identifies each pixel with a susceptibility grid weighting, where each 90-m pixel is given a weight of 0 to 81 depending on how many 10-m pixels within it are identified as susceptible to landslides. Mirus et al (2024) assigned numerical bins for grid values with the percentage of kno

given a weight of 0 to 81 depending on how many 10-m pixels within it are identified as susceptible to landslides. Mirus et al (2024) assigned numerical bins for grid values with the percentage of known historical landslides that occurred within each bin (Table 58). The percentage of total historical landslides was used to normalize the grid weights, providing a percentage of susceptibility within each pixel cell. Building value and population counts that fell within each grid bin were multiplied by the grid weight percent to identify exposed values. For instance, if a building fell within a susceptibility grid cell value of 48, then 16% of the building value would be used as susceptible. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 16-4 16.2.2. FREQUENCY PROCESSING The U.S. Geological Survey county-level annualized landslide frequencies for the continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i are presented as a rate of landslides per 1,000 square km per year for each county. These landslide frequency densities were multiplied by the county’s area in order to get the number of landslides per year for each county. The county-level estimated landslide counts were then split into Census tracts using an area-weighted percentage based on the 2024 U.S. Geological Survey Landslide Susceptibility map and the bins used to assign grid exposure weights (Table 58), allocating a fraction of the counties’ estimated landslides to each Census tract. The only deviation from this procedure within the continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i is for San Bernadino County, California (FIPS 06071). Due to the disproportionate tract sizes, building values, and susceptibility,

procedure within the continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i is for San Bernadino County, California (FIPS 06071). Due to the disproportionate tract sizes, building values, and susceptibility, the Census tract level distribution of landslide frequency was providing the largest tract in the western portion of the county with a disproportionately large fraction of the estimated yearly landslide losses, despite there being no recorded landslides occurrences in that tract. Therefore, it was decided that the frequencies would be adjusted for this county using a weighting factor of 50/50, where half the weighting used the standard methodology outlined above and the other half of the weighting used the percentage of the county’s historical landslides in each tract as documented in the 2025 U.S. Geological Survey Landslide Inventories, Version 3. For Puerto Rico, the national frequency methodology was not possible because the 2023 release of the National Seismic Hazard Model that is rel

U.S. Geological Survey Landslide Inventories, Version 3. For Puerto Rico, the national frequency methodology was not possible because the 2023 release of the National Seismic Hazard Model that is relied upon for MMI estimates does not include Puerto Rico, excluding it from analysis in the 2025 U.S. Geological Survey landslide frequency study. An alternative determination for Puerto Rico landslide frequencies was therefore needed. A vast majority of the historical count of landslide events within Puerto Rico (71,431 out of the total recorded 71,638 landslides) occurred during Hurricanes Irma and Maria. These data were collected as a part of the study of landslide susceptibility on the island of Puerto Rico (Hughes et al., 2019), and the number and spatial extent of these landslides are broadly agreed to be well documented and comprehensive by subject matter experts. Historically, landslide counts and locations in Puerto Rico are available for an extremely limited number of triggering e

adly agreed to be well documented and comprehensive by subject matter experts. Historically, landslide counts and locations in Puerto Rico are available for an extremely limited number of triggering events. Hurricanes Irma and Maria produced an unprecedented number of landslides with almost complete coverage of the island. Studies of this, and other past events, help to elucidate the frequency of widespread triggering events as well as their potential number of landslides that are possible through similar future events. The process of determining landslide frequency within Puerto Rico was done in two separate analyses. First, the landslide inventory compiled by Hughes et al. (2019) and leveraged in the Hughes and Shultz 2020 study 105 is regarded as having comprehensive and well-placed landslide points consistently across the ~71,000-point database. Landslide Historic Loss Ratio The Landslide Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to a Landslide occurrence, or the average rate of loss associated with a Landslide occurrence. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Landslide hazard type. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS107 at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated. SHELDUS events from 1996 through 2023 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation. Seven peril types are mapped to the hazard Landslide (see Table 61). Total losses due to Hurricane Maria were estimated to be approximately $90 billion across Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands.108 However, that number encompasses all losses attributed to the hurricane, not just landslide losses. Within Puerto Rico alone, there was an estimated 300,000 homes lost from hurricane, flooding, and landslides. 109 To identify only potential building losses due to landslides, the Census sub-block dasymetric polygons that also contained landslide points from Hurricane Maria (Hughes et al., 2019) were identified. ns that also contained landslide points from Hurricane Maria (Hughes et al., 2019) were identified. All known buildings within these sub-block dasymetric polygons were then identified and the building values summed to provide a potential loss of buildings by Hurricane Maria to be $6.6B. This value was then distributed across the island using a 50-50 weighting of landslide frequency and total building value within the Census tracts. The Historic Loss Ratio exposure value for Landslide is a county-level value that represents the dollar value of the total building value or the entire population of a county as recorded in Hazus 6.0. Landslide Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the u

This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  The current (2024) U.S. Geological Survey landslide susceptibility map vastly improves resolution of landslide area identification from a 1-km grid to a 90-m grid, based on down- sampling of a 10-m binary identification system (either susceptible or not). A gradational approach to landslide susceptibility which incorporates additional landslide triggering factors

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 16-19 to help identify areas which are more or less susceptible to landslides would allow for more specific estimations of exposure and losses.  The susceptible map data depicts areas of steep slopes where landslides may begin and do not represent where the landslide or debris flow may travel and deposit. While the locations are at least partially accounted for during the process of downsampling to a 90-meter resolution, explicit consideration of runout areas would improve accuracy of the loss and risk assessments.  The landslide Expected Annual Loss calculations treat all types of landslides (rock falls, debris flows, shallow vs deep landslides, among others) the same. Additional information on the predicted landslide types and characteristics could help refine the geographic extent and severity of landslide occurrences.  U.S. Geological Survey Landslide susceptibility data are currently limited to just the United States and

haracteristics could help refine the geographic extent and severity of landslide occurrences.  U.S. Geological Survey Landslide susceptibility data are currently limited to just the United States and Puerto Rico, leaving the other territories (American Samoa, Guam, U.S. Virgin Islands, Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands) out of the Landslide risk assessment. Territorial inclusion is contingent on the availability of 10-m DEM data. Once the high-resolution DEM data is available for the other territories, it is hoped that the landslide susceptibility and frequency estimates could be extended to include them.  The new landslide frequency identification is limited in scope to just the continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i. It is hoped for future releases to include Puerto Rico and the remaining territories (contingent on the inclusion of susceptibility data and seismic hazard models).  The landslide frequency estimates consider spatially homogeneous triggering thresho

Rico and the remaining territories (contingent on the inclusion of susceptibility data and seismic hazard models).  The landslide frequency estimates consider spatially homogeneous triggering thresholds, rather than probabilities of landslide triggering that vary spatially and with increasing magnitude of the triggering event. Future iterations could consider that increasing storm severity and ground motion would have increasing likelihood of landslide occurrence with increased numbers of landslides, and that these relationships vary spatially.  Landslide susceptibility and frequency identification in the Continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i primarily considers seismically induced landslides and does not explicitly consider rainfall, whereas the current Puerto Rico susceptibility and frequency calculations are based solely on weather-induced landslides. Ideally in future iterations, a consistent and comprehensive assessment of triggering events (earthquakes, rainfall, volca

ty and frequency calculations are based solely on weather-induced landslides. Ideally in future iterations, a consistent and comprehensive assessment of triggering events (earthquakes, rainfall, volcanic activity, among others) would be considered across the United States and its territories.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 17-1 17. Lightning Lightning is a visible electrical discharge or spark of electricity in the atmosphere between clouds, the air, and/or the ground often produced by a thunderstorm. The Lightning hazard encompasses the Continental United States, Alaska, Hawai‘i, and Puerto Rico, but does not include the remaining Territories due to insufficient data. Unlike other hazards, Lightning modeling has required greater attention to the treatment and interpretation of annualized frequency. The Lightning hazard only calculates risk for the Building and Population consequence types. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 17.1. Lightning Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Source: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration/National Weather Service/Storm Prediction Center and Vaisala NLDN, Daily Lightning Climatology112 The Storm Prediction Center currently

currence Source: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration/National Weather Service/Storm Prediction Center and Vaisala NLDN, Daily Lightning Climatology112 The Storm Prediction Center currently maintains a dataset providing yearly gridded Lightning climatologies derived using historical records of daily cloud-to-ground strikes over the Continental United States, Alaska, Hawai‘i, and Puerto Rico. Spatiotemporal records are in NetCDF (Network Common Data Form) format for 366 days (including Leap Year). Separate datasets were provided for the Continental United States, Alaska, Hawai‘i, and Puerto Rico, with slight variations in spatial resolution and climatologies across the three datasets. For the Continental United States and Alaska, each layer is a grid of 40-by-40-km cells in the Albers Equal Area projection (see Figure 82); for Hawai‘i and Puerto Rico, data are presented in a 3-by-3-km grid. The Continental United States annual probabilities represent the percent days per year

lbers Equal Area projection (see Figure 82); for Hawai‘i and Puerto Rico, data are presented in a 3-by-3-km grid. The Continental United States annual probabilities represent the percent days per year that an area within that grid cell would expect to experience at least one Lightning strike based on a 29-year period of record (1995-2024). Outside the Continental United States annual climatology is the true annual likelihood of Lightning, derived over a 12-year period of record (2012-2024). These yearly values are used to calculate annualized frequency at the Census block level. 112 NCEI, NOAA. (2017). Cloud-to-ground lightning strikes, Prototype [online dataset]. Retrieved from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/lightning-products.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 17-2 Fi gure 82: Map of Lightning Strikes 17.2. Lightning Spatial Data Processing The latitude and longitude values and the cloud-to-ground flash threshold of each entry in the NetCDF file containing gridded annual Lightning climatology data were extracted to points. These points were then buffered by 20km for the Continental United States and Alaska. Points in Hawai‘i and Puerto Rico were buffered by 1.5km. After the buffer, a minimum bounding geometry (envelope) was created around each point. The result was a gridded polygon dataset containing the mean percent days per year bucketed by the number of cloud-to-ground flashes per hour that were observed within the buffer of a given grid point. These polygons in the processing database could then be intersected with the Census-block polygons to calculate annualized frequency and exposure. 17.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Lightning can occur almost anywhere

e could then be intersected with the Census-block polygons to calculate annualized frequency and exposure. 17.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Lightning can occur almost anywhere under the right conditions, so all counties were deemed possible for Lightning strike occurrence. While the current data source does not supply information for the Pacific Territories (Guam, Northern Mariana Islands, American Samoa) or the U.S. Virgin Islands, these areas are still included as possible for Lightning occurrence. No Scores are available for Guam, Mariana Islands, American Samoa, or the U.S. Virgin Islands as the data are insufficient.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 17-3 17.3. Lightning Non-Spatial Data Processing 17.3.1. PERIOD OF RECORD The Storm Prediction Center Daily Lightning Climatology data incorporate Lightning strikes observed between Jan. 01, 1995 and Dec. 31, 2023 for the Continental United States and between Jan. 01, 2012 and Dec. 31, 2024 for Outside the Continental United States, so the period of record for which Lightning data are utilized is 29 years and 12 years, respectively. 17.4. Lightning Exposure Because Lightning strikes can occur anywhere, the entire building and population value of a Census block, Census tract, and county are considered exposed to Lightning. Population equivalence, which is used in select Expected Annual Loss calculations, is calculated by multiplying population by the Value of Statistical Life ($13.7 million per person). 17.5. Lightning Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the estimated number of days with a recorded Ligh

population by the Value of Statistical Life ($13.7 million per person). 17.5. Lightning Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the estimated number of days with a recorded Lightning strike each year for a specific area. The annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block-level value as an area-weighted aggregation of the annualized frequencies of its intersecting polygons obtained from the source data. The Census block value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. To calculate annualized frequency at the Census block level, the gridded Lightning polygons obtained from the source data are first intersected with the Census block polygons within the processing database. The resulting table contains the polygon’s unique identifier, Census block number, and the intersected area in square kilometers (see Table 64). urrent methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  Reliable data sources for Lightning strikes with a sufficient history are difficult to come by for some United States Territories. As such, the source hazard data for the Lightning hazard are still limited to the Continental United States, Alaska, Hawai‘i, and Puerto Rico, leaving American Samoa, Guam, Northern Mariana Islands, and the U.S. Virgin Islands out of the risk calculations for this hazard.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 18-1 18. Strong Wind Strong Wind consists of damaging winds, often originating from thunderstorms, which are classified as exceeding 58 mph. Losses due to Strong Wind are modeled using Severe Weather Database Files data from the Storm Prediction Center and SHELDUS. The Severe Weather Database Files provide historical records of Strong Wind events, which are used for estimating event extents, calculating the historical occurrence, and calculating the frequency and exposure components. SHELDUS is used for historical losses, which are used to build the Historic Loss Ratio. The Strong Wind hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 18.1. se see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 18.1. Strong Wind Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Source: National Weather Service, Storm Prediction Center, Severe Weather Database Files114 The Storm Prediction Center compiles all records of damaging Wind from the National Weather Service’s monthly Storm Data publication and makes them available in CSV format on the Severe Weather Maps, Graphics, and Data Page website. These files record spatiotemporal information (start and end coordinates, date, time) as well as economic loss, wind speed in knots, and, from 2006 on, whether the wind speed was measured or estimated and whether the speed denotes a gust wind speed or a sustained wind speed (see Table 67 and Figure 87). Strong Wind Spatial Data Processing The source data includes fields for two sets of coordinates, a start, and an end. This is mainly because the data share their format with the data for tornadoes. Most Wind events only have start coordinates (or the end coordinates match the start coordinates), so the points are projected from these coordinates. Any events outside of the period of record or with wind speeds of less than 50.4 knots (58 mph)115 were filtered out. An 80-km buffer was created from the remaining points to produce a layer of Strong Wind event polygons (see Figure 88). The 80-km buffer is not an attempt to represent the area of impact by a Strong Wind event, but rather an effort to estimate the area where Strong Winds may have been present. The Strong Wind event polygons could then be used to estimate annualized frequency at the Census block level. 115 This threshold is used

to estimate the area where Strong Winds may have been present. The Strong Wind event polygons could then be used to estimate annualized frequency at the Census block level. 115 This threshold is used by National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and the National Weather Service as the minimum wind gust criterion for a Severe Thunderstorm Watch.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 18-3 Fi gure 88: Map of Buffered Wind Points 18.2.1. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Strong Winds can occur almost anywhere under the right conditions, so all counties were deemed possible for Strong Wind occurrence. 18.3. Strong Wind Non-Spatial Data Processing 18.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Strong Wind, in events, is initially computed as the number of distinct Strong Wind event polygons that intersect a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid cell. Buffering the Strong Wind points and using the fishnet grid to count historical Strong Wind events serves to spatially spread the influence of past Strong Wind events to nearby areas that may also be susceptible to Strong Wind but have not experienced Strong Wind as frequently. However, using these methods can overestimate Strong Wind frequency. To adjust for this, a national scaling factor is calculated (see Equation 108). Equation 108: N

erienced Strong Wind as frequently. However, using these methods can overestimate Strong Wind frequency. To adjust for this, a national scaling factor is calculated (see Equation 108). Equation 108: National Scaling Factor for Strong Wind Event Count

This annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level, and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations use the Strong Wind event polygons created from the source data (as described in Section 18.2 Strong Wind Spatial Data Processing), as well as their corresponding computed duration days from the pr

Strong Wind Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  Due to the inexact differentiation of Strong Wind, Hurricane Wind, and Tornado-caused damage for historical win

n 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  Due to the inexact differentiation of Strong Wind, Hurricane Wind, and Tornado-caused damage for historical wind events, the data development team recognizes that the underlying data may combine losses from different strong wind impacts. Additionally, many hazards are exacerbated by the impacts of strong winds, including wildfire and drought, among others.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 19-1 19. Tornado A Tornado is a narrow, violently rotating column of air that extends from the base of a thunderstorm to the ground and is visible only if it forms a condensation funnel made up of water droplets, dust, and debris. The Tornado hazard is a complex model that involves subtype distinctions that are processed separately before being combined into the overall Tornado hazard. These subtypes are based on the severity of the Tornado event. The model uses the Severe Weather Database Files data from the Storm Prediction Center and SHELDUS as its data sources. The Severe Weather Database Files provide historical records of Tornado events, which are used for establishing the three subtypes, calculating the historical occurrence of each subtype, and calculating the frequency and exposure components. SHELDUS is used for historical losses, which are used to build the Historic Loss Ratio. The Tornado hazard calculates risk for a

of each subtype, and calculating the frequency and exposure components. SHELDUS is used for historical losses, which are used to build the Historic Loss Ratio. The Tornado hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 19.1. Tornado Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Source: National Weather Service, Storm Prediction Center, Severe Weather Database Files118 The Storm Prediction Center compiles all records of Tornadoes from the National Weather Service’s monthly Storm Data publication and makes them available in CSV and shapefile format on the Severe Weather Maps, Graphics, and Data Page website. Shapefiles representing Tornadoes as both points (initial touchdown points) and lines (paths) were downloaded. These files record spatiotemporal information (start and end coordinates, date, time) as well as economic loss, injur

adoes as both points (initial touchdown points) and lines (paths) were downloaded. These files record spatiotemporal information (start and end coordinates, date, time) as well as economic loss, injuries, fatalities, and, depending on the date of the Tornado, Fujita (F-) or EF- scale category (see Table 70 and Figure 95). Economic loss information is recorded as either a predefined category of loss (1950- 1995), a value representing loss in millions of dollars (1996-2015), or the loss in dollars (2016- present). Tornado records with two distinct sets of start and end coordinates represent a Tornado path. A record with identical start and end coordinates or with no end coordinates represents a Tornado touchdown. 118 SPC, NWS. (2024). Severe Weather Database files, Tornado, 1950-2023 [online dataset]. Retrieved from http://www.spc.noaa.gov/wcm/.

With the intended spatial processing goal of intersecting Tornado events to determine the Census block (and parent county) that the Tornado traversed, Tornado path lines and touchdown points were buffered to create tornado event path polygons. To conservatively estimate the largest area for each Tornado polygon, even those without a complete path geometry from the source data, three methods were used to calculate a possible buffer radius. Whichever method yielded the largest radius was used to buffer the given line or point. ing historical Tornado event counts at the Census tract and county level. Because Tornado occurrences are recorded at distinct locations and multiple Tornadoes are often reported on the same day in near proximity, it was necessary for annualized frequency estimation to spread the influence of the reported historical event. Thus, an additional 80-km buffer was created from these category-buffered polygons (see Figure 96). This 80-km buffer radius is not an attempt to represent the Tornado’s impact area. Rather, it is better to represent the area where the event could possibly have occurred. The 80-km buffered Tornado event path polygons are intersected with the 49-by-49-km fishnet grid and used to estimate annualized frequency at the Census tract level.

equency. 19.3.2. PERIOD OF RECORD Tornado data between Jan. 01, 1950 and Dec. 31, 2023 are analyzed, so the period of record for which Tornado data are utilized is 74.0 years. The entire 74-year period of record is utilized for tornadoes in EF-scale categories 2, 3, 4, and 5 while tornado data between Jan. 01, 1986 and Dec. 31, 2023 (38.0 years) are utilized for tornadoes in EF-scale categories 0 and 1.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 19-5 19.4. Tornado Exposure The size of the damage area caused by a Tornado can vary greatly depending on its magnitude or EF- scale. For this reason, exposure is calculated for three sub-types: 1) EF-scale 0 and 1; 2) EF-scale 2 and 3; and 3) EF-scale 4 and 5. An average historical Tornado damage area is assigned to each Tornado sub-type (see Table 72). These average damage area values were calculated from the historical set of Tornado event path polygons generated according to one of the three methods described in Section 19.2 Tornado Spatial Data Processing and not from the subsequent 80-km buffer applied to the Tornado event path polygon used for frequency estimation. Tornado Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the estimated number of recorded Tornado occurrences, in events, each year for a specific area. Annualized frequency is calculated for each Tornado sub- type at the county and Census tract levels, which are used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations are determined by intersecting the 80-km buffered Tornado event path polygons generated in Section 19.2 Tornado Spatial Data Processing with a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid. The count of distinct Tornado event path polygons of ea

ecting the 80-km buffered Tornado event path polygons generated in Section 19.2 Tornado Spatial Data Processing with a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid. The count of distinct Tornado event path polygons of each sub-type intersecting each grid cell is recorded (see Appendix D – Fishnet Occurrence Count). Buffering the Tornado paths and using the 49-by-49-km fishnet grid to count historical Tornado events serves to spatially spread the influence of past events to nearby areas that may also be susceptible to Tornadoes but have not experienced as many. However, using these methods can overestimate Tornado annualized frequency. To adjust for this, a national scaling factor is calculated for each Tornado sub-type (see Equation 117). This scaling factor is set to 1/932 or one divided by the total count of all 49-by-49-km fishnet grid cells that do not intersect a historical Tornado path but intersect one or more United States counties (see Table 73).

kilometers; however, the land area a grid cell covers varies by location. Tornado Historic Loss Ratio The Tornado Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to a Tornado occurrence, or the average rate of loss associated with a Tornado occurrence. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Tornado hazard type. Unique to the Tornado hazard, individual Historic Loss Ratios were calculated for each severity sub-type within each consequence type to reflect the diverse levels of destruction that can occur across the EF scale. Unlike other hazards in this analysis, the overall consequence-specific Historic Loss Ratios are determined through a back-calculation from the final Expected Annual Loss value, as discussed later in this section. Loss data are provided b

the overall consequence-specific Historic Loss Ratios are determined through a back-calculation from the final Expected Annual Loss value, as discussed later in this section. Loss data are provided by SHELDUS119 at the county level, so this is the lowest level at which Historic Loss Ratio is calculated for each of the individual Tornado sub-types. SHELDUS events from 1996 through 2019 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation. Five peril types are mapped to the hazard Tornado (see Table 74). Native records of Tornadoes that caused loss over more than one day (such as those that occurred overnight) have their loss assigned to the first day (see Section 5.4.4 Historic Loss Ratio Calculation Methodology). 119 For Tornado loss information, SHELDUS compiles data from the monthly Storm Data publication produced by National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s National Centers for Environmental Information.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 19-14 Table 74: Tornado Peril Types and Recorded Events from 1996-2023 Peril Type in SHELDUS Total SHELDUS Loss Records Total Records per Event Basis Fire-Tornado 0 2 Tornado 20,408 48,421 Waterspout 10 534 Wind-Tornadic 0 46 Wind-Vortex 2 16 Tornado E xpected Annual Loss calculations require sub-type specific Historic Loss Ratio values. To accomplish this, native SHELDUS loss records are matched to specific Tornados, so that each Loss Ratio per Basis is calculated using loss attributed to a specific Tornado and its exposure within a county as determined by the tornado event-path polygon and Census block intersects. In most cases, a single Tornado event occurs on a single day in a single county. However, multiple Tornadoes can occur in the same county on the same day, and a single Tornado may cause damage in multiple counties. To make this matching as precise as possible, several strategies were implemented. SHELDUS Tornado reco

in the same county on the same day, and a single Tornado may cause damage in multiple counties. To make this matching as precise as possible, several strategies were implemented. SHELDUS Tornado records from 2000 forward typically include a unique identifier to link them to records in the National Centers for Environmental Information Storm Events Database. The National Centers for Environmental Information records have additional information that is not in the SHELDUS data but is often present in the Tornado spatial source data. This includes event timestamps, EF-scale, length and width of the Tornado path, begin and end coordinates of the path, and the full Tornado event loss data. A fuzzy logic approach was used to map as many historical Tornado paths as possible to their county-specific loss in SHELDUS using these fields held in common between the spatial source data and National Centers for Environmental Information data. For example, it could be assumed that if a Tornado path re

ic loss in SHELDUS using these fields held in common between the spatial source data and National Centers for Environmental Information data. For example, it could be assumed that if a Tornado path record occurred on the same day with the same timestamp and had the same recorded EF-scale, physical dimensions, and geographic coordinates as a National Centers for Environmental Information record, both sources were describing the same Tornado event. This approach was iterated multiple times, each time using less stringent requirements for matching. By matching a National Centers for Environmental Information record to a Tornado path, the exposure of a specific Tornado within a county could be matched to its SHELDUS county loss data. Tornadoes with an EF-scale of 4 or 5 are rare and have much larger damage areas. They often occur during particularly bad storms, which can spawn multiple Tornadoes and can have long paths that impact multiple counties. Some of these Tornadoes may not be match

larger damage areas. They often occur during particularly bad storms, which can spawn multiple Tornadoes and can have long paths that impact multiple counties. Some of these Tornadoes may not be matched in the source data using the fuzzy logic approach, which could reduce their impact in the Historic Loss Ratio and give an inaccurate estimate of Expected Annual Loss. A decision was made to manually inspect Tornado records of this sub-type and ensure that the loss of each of these Tornado events was appropriately matched to the correct path.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 19-15 The Tornado match results were reviewed and analyzed. The aggregated total loss (from the multiple county SHELDUS loss records attributed to the same Tornado event) was compared to the loss reported for the tornado path in the source data (from the Storm Prediction Center). When averaging the ratio of SHELDUS loss to Tornado path loss (from the Storm Prediction Center) among the matched records, a strong correlation is seen (see Table 75). Tornado Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  The Tornado hazard is a complex model that involves subtype distinctions that are processed separately before being combined in

s and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  The Tornado hazard is a complex model that involves subtype distinctions that are processed separately before being combined into the overall Tornado hazard. These subtypes are based on the severity of the Tornado event. As a result of this sub-hazard approach, the published frequency and Historic Loss Ratio values are surrogates back calculated from the final Expected Annual Loss values. As such, this is one of the few hazards in this analysis that has Census tract-level Historic Loss Ratios.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 20-1 20. Tsunami A Tsunami is a wave or series of waves generated by gravitational displacements of bodies of water. These displacements can be caused by earthquakes, landslides, volcanic eruptions, or even large meteors hitting the ocean and causing a rise or mounding of water at the ocean surface. A Tsunami can travel across the open ocean at about 500 mph and slow down to about 30 mph as it approaches land, causing it to grow significantly in height. The data development team collaborated with the National Tsunami Hazard Mitigation Program within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and U.S. Geological Survey to replace Expected Annual Loss data for Tsunami with results based on the best available hazard data from high-risk tsunami states and territories (Alaska, American Samoa, California, Guam, Hawai‘i, Northern Mariana Islands, Oregon, Puerto Rico, Washington and U.S. Virgin Islands). These new data are stan

high-risk tsunami states and territories (Alaska, American Samoa, California, Guam, Hawai‘i, Northern Mariana Islands, Oregon, Puerto Rico, Washington and U.S. Virgin Islands). These new data are standardized and processed according to guidance from the National Tsunami Hazard Mitigation Program and U.S. Geological Survey to best estimate the risk to buildings and population. The Tsunami hazard calculates risk for only the Building and Population consequence types. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 20.1. Tsunami Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Source: National Geophysical Data Center, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, National Centers for Environmental Information/World Data Service Global Historical Tsunami Database The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration maintains a database of historical Tsunami runup points with records of Tsunami events dating back to 1800. These re

bal Historical Tsunami Database The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration maintains a database of historical Tsunami runup points with records of Tsunami events dating back to 1800. These records supply spatiotemporal information, including geographic coordinates, observation date, and tsunami event validity, and occasionally some information on magnitude (like water height) or damage, such as deaths, injuries, and destruction to property. Each runup point has a unique identifier and each Tsunami originating event also has a unique identifier. Each Tsunami event typically causes multiple runup events. Runup points are available for download in CSV format (see Table 77). One known limitation of this dataset is that there are fewer gauges in Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands; therefore, historical runup events in these locations may be underrepresented. Loss Quantification Source: The Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Estimated Average Annualized Tsunami Losses for the United States (P-2426) Starting in 2023, the Federal Emergency Management Agency collaborated with the National Tsunami Hazard Mitigation Program, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and the U.S. Geological Survey to provide a comprehensive national assessment of tsunami risk. It includes earthquake-induced tsunami risk but does not include impacts from tsunamis generated by landslides, volcanic eruptions, glacial calving, near-earth objects, weather, or other events. t includes earthquake-induced tsunami risk but does not include impacts from tsunamis generated by landslides, volcanic eruptions, glacial calving, near-earth objects, weather, or other events. The results of the study provide economic loss data to buildings (structural, non-structural, contents, and inventory replacement values) and residential populations from modeled earthquake-generated tsunami scenarios. This study is based on the best available hazard data from the United States West Coast (California, Oregon, and Washington), Alaska, Hawai‘i, United States Pacific Territories (American Samoa, Guam, and the Northern Mariana Islands) and Caribbean Territories (Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands). DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE This dataset used P-2426’s results as the basis to determine the areas where Tsunami occurrence is possible, where applicable. The dataset’s criteria for inclusion were if a Census tract is within 300 feet of the coast, or if it is further than 300 feet from the coast but has an average annualized loss value. The development team reviewed this initial set of Census tracts with Subject Matter Experts to determine if any modifications should be made to the hazard occurrence areas. The results of this discussion yielded that all Census blocks using this method would be included except for those upriver of St. Hel

f any modifications should be made to the hazard occurrence areas. The results of this discussion yielded that all Census blocks using this method would be included except for those upriver of St. Helens, Oregon. In addition to the inclusion criteria above, regional guidance was used to determine hazard possibility for the East, Gulf, and Great Lakes coasts which were outside the bounds of P-2426.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 20-4 For the East Coast, any Census block in a county that was included in the U.S. Geological Survey’s Estimates of population exposure and evacuation potential for earthquake-related tsunami hazard zones in the United States report was deemed as hazard possible.121 In the Gulf Coast (Texas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, and western Florida), any Census block in a county was designated as hazard possible when the county directly touched the waters of the Gulf, as these areas are threatened by submarine landslide sources in the Gulf. In Louisiana, the parishes of Tangipahoa, St. John the Baptist, and St. Charles were also marked as hazard possible, as they border Lake Pontchartrain, where a seiche event was observed in 2003. 122 Along the Great Lakes coast, any Census block located in a Census tract bordering a Great Lake, or that was deemed possible for Coastal Flooding (see Section 7.2), is included as one in which tsunami-

Along the Great Lakes coast, any Census block located in a Census tract bordering a Great Lake, or that was deemed possible for Coastal Flooding (see Section 7.2), is included as one in which tsunami-like events are considered possible. In further consultations with Subject Matter Experts, it was determined that additional refinement of these counts was required to incorporate the state-to-state or regional variability of tsunami assessments and conceptualization. Tsunami Annualized Frequency The Tsunami annualized frequency value is composed of two values: one frequency based on historical counts of events; another frequency derived from the probabilistic tsunami return periods used by P-2426 for its impact calculations. The annualized frequency value provided to the user is a summation of these two frequencies strictly as a surrogate value meant to represent the annualized frequency of tsunamis in a way that incorporates both the historical and probabilistic components of the tsunami hazard. This surrogate frequency is the figure presented in the dataset. The first value is based on the regional Tsunami runup-event count value data discusse

probabilistic components of the tsunami hazard. This surrogate frequency is the figure presented in the dataset. The first value is based on the regional Tsunami runup-event count value data discussed in Section 20.2.2 Historical Occurrence Count. This Regional event count was then divided by the historical event database’s period of record (222.15 years) to create the representative annualized frequency of historical events within that Region. This value was then assigned to all counties, Census tracts, and Census blocks within that Region, meaning all Census blocks within a Tsunami Region contain the same historical event annualized frequency, but this value is unique for each of the 17 Tsunami Regions. This value is not used in any calculations or results within the dataset and is merely an indicator for the user’s reference. Volcanic Activity Volcanic Activity occurs via vents that act as conduits between the Earth’s surface and inner layers, producing the eruption of materials such as gas, molten rock, and volcanic ash when gas pressure and buoyancy drive molten rock upward and through zones of weakness in the Earth’s crust. The Volcanic Activity hazard considers direct impacts from explosive eruptions, pyroclastic density currents, lava flows, debris avalanches, lahars, and ashfall. The data development team engaged in extensive collaboration with subject matter experts to develop susceptibility and impact area layers for volcanoes. Susceptibility zones were determined based on two sub-hazard categories, Near- Volcano (including lahars), and Ashfall. Near-Volcano susceptibility was determined based on the U.S. Geological Survey’s National Volcanic Threat Layer and 2018 Update to the U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Assessme

Ashfall. Near-Volcano susceptibility was determined based on the U.S. Geological Survey’s National Volcanic Threat Layer and 2018 Update to the U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Assessment datasets. For the Ashfall sub-hazard a susceptibility layer was developed with input for U.S. Geological Survey subject matter experts. The Volcanic Activity hazard calculates risk only for the Building and Population consequence types. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 21.1. Volcanic Activity Spatial Source Data Susceptible Area Source: U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Layer123 The U.S. Geological Survey’s National Volcanic Threat Layer identifies 161 active or potentially active volcanos within the United States and its territories. The layer depicts areas near the volcanic vents that could be affected by proximal volcanic hazards including airborne debris from explosive eruptions (otherwise

the United States and its territories. The layer depicts areas near the volcanic vents that could be affected by proximal volcanic hazards including airborne debris from explosive eruptions (otherwise known as ballistics), pyroclastic density currents, lava flows, debris avalanches, lahars, and heavy ashfall. Where applicable, it also shows areas downstream that could be affected by distal lahar flows that have the potential to extend beyond this proximal hazard boundary. Volcanic Activity Spatial Data Processing Volcanic Activity was broken down into two sub-hazards during processing due to the significantly different aerial extents and historic loss ratios of the two sub-hazards. These sub-hazards are termed “Near-Volcano” and “Ashfall.” 21.2.1. NEAR-VOLCANO SUB-HAZARD EXPOSURE DEFINITION The Near-Volcano hazard is sourced from the U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Layer, which contains two separate hazard identifications, near-volcano (which includes ballistics, pyr

TION The Near-Volcano hazard is sourced from the U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Layer, which contains two separate hazard identifications, near-volcano (which includes ballistics, pyroclastic density currents, lava flows, debris avalanches, and heavy ashfall) and lahar. Both were treated the same within the Near-Volcano sub-hazard. The U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Layer shapefiles were then used to identify Census blocks, Census tracts, and counties susceptible to Near-Volcanic hazards. 21.2.2. ASHFALL EXPOSURE DETERMINATION Though the National Volcanic Threat Layer captures most of the significant impacts to buildings and people due to volcanic activity, it currently does not account for Ashfall beyond the boundaries of the National Volcanic Threat Layer in its hazard extents. Ash from a volcanic eruption can travel for hundreds, sometimes thousands of kilometers from the eruption source, potentially damaging buildings, disrupting transportation cor

r in its hazard extents. Ash from a volcanic eruption can travel for hundreds, sometimes thousands of kilometers from the eruption source, potentially damaging buildings, disrupting transportation corridors, contaminating water supplies, and impacting air quality. According to estimates, structural building damage like what is measured in this analysis requires approximately 50 to 100 mm of Ashfall. 134 While some existing data on Ashfall extents do exist, these datasets do not reflect Ashfall in terms that allow for direct building and population losses to be measured. To account for this data gap, a simplified Ashfall model was created for this analysis. The generalized model presented here is a first step in representing the potential Ashfall impacts in counties outside the Near-Volcano sub-hazard area as defined by the National Volcano Threat Layer. 133 New Zealand Defence Force (NZDF). National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 21-6 After discussions with subject matter experts, it was decided that the Ashfall sub-hazard would employ a buffer approach based on observed historical events and prevailing wind direction. Wind rose data at 0 to 5 km elevation above each volcano were used to determine generalized Ashfall sub-hazard extents. A minimum buffer was set at 100km based on expert recommendation for a likely event area and to account for variability in wind direction and speed throughout a given year. Each volcano’s wind rose informed the general shape of the Ashfall buffer, with a maximum radius of 250km applied along the axis of prevailing wind direction to capture most of the counties that have historically experienced Ashfall losses. The resulting polygons were merged to create a single ashfall susceptibility area, and the outer extent was smoothed using a 75km tolerance. The National Volcanic Threat Layer polygons were then clipped out to avoid

olygons were merged to create a single ashfall susceptibility area, and the outer extent was smoothed using a 75km tolerance. The National Volcanic Threat Layer polygons were then clipped out to avoid duplication as heavy ashfall is already accounted for in that layer. If counties with historical ashfall losses within the SHELDUS database were not included within the generated Ashfall susceptibility cloud, the individual volcano’s Ashfall “cloud” was extended to include those counties. For example, in 2001, Puerto Rico experienced historical SHELDUS ashfall losses from the Soufrière Hills volcano in Montserrat, approximately 475km away from the furthest listed county, so a 475km buffer was added to the volcano and all counties within it were identified as susceptible to Ashfall. See Figure 109 for a visualization of this process and for the resulting Ashfall susceptibility for all volcanoes. Fi gure 109: Ashfall Susceptibility Development

DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE The spatial areas with the potential to be impacted by volcanic events were identified using the U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Layer and the generated Ashfall sub-hazard areas. To distinguish between areas where no Volcanic Activity has occurred and those where such events are not deemed possible, a control table was generated to designate which counties have some probability of Volcanic Activity. Any county with a Census block that intersected one or more of the identified volcanic threat areas is included as one in which Volcanic Activity is possible (see Figure 111).
