Volcanic Activity Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency for Volcanic Activity is exceptionally low when compared to other hazard types. Although the known and dated eruption histories for some volcanoes go back millions of years, and sometimes hundreds of millions of years, the details of volcanic eruption events within

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 21-11 the last few thousands of years are lacking for most volcanoes within the National Volcanic Threat Layer. However, one of the factors within the 24-factor hazard and exposure matrix of the 2018 update to the U.S. Geological Survey National Volcanic Threat Assessment is “Eruption recurrence”, which ranks the volcanoes on a scale of 0 to 4, with increasing factor values associated with decreasing recurrence intervals (Table 80). Table 80: National Volcanic Threat Assessment Eruption Recurrence Interval Within the matrix, the eruption recurrence factor was determined as a time range due to limited data, where few volcanoes have enough dated historical eruptions events to provide an accurate recurrence interval, and the bimodal nature of volcanic eruptions produces clusters of frequent eruptions that are separated by longer periods of quiescence. 135 From discussions with the U.S. Geological Survey, it was determined that the

ture of volcanic eruptions produces clusters of frequent eruptions that are separated by longer periods of quiescence. 135 From discussions with the U.S. Geological Survey, it was determined that the high end of each estimation range would be ideal for use in frequency estimates for each volcanic hazard. For instance, if a volcanic hazard was labeled with a recurrence interval Hazard Score of 4, it is estimated to have another volcanic eruption within the next 1 to 99 years. For these volcanoes, a recurrence interval of 99 years is used, calculated as an annual estimated volcanic hazard risk of 1/99 = 0.0101. This is based on an analysis of the Holocene (the last ~11,000 years) eruption history from the Smithsonian Institute’s Global Volcanism Program 136 to determine average eruption intervals within recent geological history. The only addition to these annual frequencies is the inclusion of Montserrat’s Soufrière Hills volcano that caused ashfall losses in the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico. Since Soufrière Hills is not in the National Volcanic Threat Layer, there is no eruption recurrence interval assigned to it. Therefore, based on the activity of the volcano, discussions with subject matter experts, and comparisons to volcanoes within the National Volcanic Threat Layer, it was determined to use an annualized frequency that matched that of the most active volcanoes currently in the National Volcanic Threat Layer (0.0101). Annualized frequency calculations use the same intersection between volcano polygons and Census block polygons that were used to calculate exposure. 21.4.1. ANNUALIZED FREQUENCY AGGREGATION The dataset presents annualized frequency values at both the Census tract and county level. The annualized frequency values at the Census block level are aggregated to the Census tract and county levels by carrying the volcanic activity annualized frequency upwards from Census block to Census tract, to county. This means that whatever the volcanic activity frequency of the Census block is, that same frequency is used for the Census tract and the county. When multipl

Census block to Census tract, to county. This means that whatever the volcanic activity frequency of the Census block is, that same frequency is used for the Census tract and the county. When multiple volcanic hazards impact Census tracts and counties, the annualized frequencies for each individual impacting volcano are added together, as described in Equation 137. Volcanic Activity Historic Loss Ratio The Volcanic Activity Historic Loss Ratio is the representative percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to a volcanic occurrence, or the average rate of loss associated with the volcanic occurrence. For a detailed description of the Historic Loss Ratio calculation

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 21-14 process, see Section 5.4. The Historic Loss Ratio parameters described below are specific to the Volcanic Activity hazard type. Most databases of volcanic losses contained either outdated data or were missing key events, so a comprehensive search for all volcanic losses attributed to volcanoes within the Unites States was conducted. Historic Loss Ratio estimates were broken down into three separate regional categories: Hawai‘i, Cascadia, and the Pacific Territories (see Figure 113). All volcanic loss events from 1960 to 2023 are included in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation. SHELDUS was used as a baseline for loss estimates and research was done to expand or adjust any listed losses. 21.5.1. NEAR-VOLCANO SUB-HAZARD HISTORIC LOSS RATIO A comprehensive evaluation of the eruption losses identified within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration National Centers for Environmental Information/World Data Service Glo

LOSS RATIO A comprehensive evaluation of the eruption losses identified within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration National Centers for Environmental Information/World Data Service Global Significant Volcanic Eruptions Database was performed to review source data and loss estimates. Further research was performed to identify impactful eruptions not included within the National Centers for Environmental Information/World Data Service database. Subject matter experts were consulted to help clarify inconsistencies among records. Once compiled and verified, the losses were then divided by exposure area. Hawai‘i: All volcanic activity losses for Hawai‘i occurred from eruptions of Kīlauea, located on the southeastern shore of Hawai‘i Island. Building exposure was defined using a map of Kīlauea’s historic 1790-2023 lava flows in order to exclude buildings outside the likely flow path. After consultation with the U.S. Geological Survey, a 1-km buffer was added to these lava flo

map of Kīlauea’s historic 1790-2023 lava flows in order to exclude buildings outside the likely flow path. After consultation with the U.S. Geological Survey, a 1-km buffer was added to these lava flows and the building exposure within that area was used for Historic Loss Ratio calculations. Cascadia: The only historical eruption in the United States to cause non-ashfall losses outside of Hawai‘i was the May 18, 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens. Initial estimates placed total losses from the eruption at $2B, though over time that value has been amended to approximately $1.1B. Data from Oregon State University citing the Washington State Department of Commerce and Economic Development Research Division divides out the losses from Mount St. Helens by category. 137 In it, the total structural losses are listed as $106.9 million, about 11% of the total losses. This value was divided by the building exposure within the National Volcanic Threat Layer’s identified hazard area for Mount St. H

l losses are listed as $106.9 million, about 11% of the total losses. This value was divided by the building exposure within the National Volcanic Threat Layer’s identified hazard area for Mount St. Helens. Two subsequent eruptions (May 25, 1980 and June 12, 1980) caused Ashfall losses in northwestern Oregon. Pacific Territories: As there are currently no loss events within the Pacific Territories due to volcanic activity, the nearby eruption of Hunga volcano in Tonga was suggested by subject matter experts as a proxy for territories of the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands and American Samoa 137 https://volcano.oregonstate.edu/cost-volcanic-eruptions

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 21-15 (Guam does not have any active volcanoes). A previous study by Weir et al. (2024)138 identified a range of Tongan loss ratios depending on particulate matter and modeling used. This data was then compared to loss estimates for the eruption divided by the total building exposure of the island. This loss ratio was within the range of the Weir et al (2024) estimates and was therefore used for the Pacific Territories’ Historic Loss Ratios. 21.5.2. ASHFALL SUB-HAZARD HISTORIC LOSS RATIO For the Ashfall sub-hazard, a uniform, minimal Historic Loss Ratio was applied across the Ashfall susceptibility layer based on historic losses recorded in SHELDUS for the two Ashfall events from Mount St. Helens (see Table 81). 21.5.3. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 21-22 21.7. Volcanic Activity Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  This methodology includes ashfall in its assessment of volcanic activity impacts. However, the nature of Ashfall damages – which can be best characterized by indirect damages including supply chain disruptions and long-term health impacts -- mostly falls outside the scope of this analysis, which focuses on direct damages to buildings and immediate population losses. The decision to include Ashfall as a sub-hazard came after extensive conversations with subject matter experts and other stakeholders, who felt it was best to acknowledge the potential for Ashfall impacts in order to

include Ashfall as a sub-hazard came after extensive conversations with subject matter experts and other stakeholders, who felt it was best to acknowledge the potential for Ashfall impacts in order to keep communities better informed of their hazard and risk profiles. The data development team acknowledges that Ashfall losses modeled in this analysis cover only a fraction of the most notable impacts, and those who would use these data for this purpose should not strictly rely on it and will need to seek additional data or resources.  Ashfall modeling data which would more accurately identify the potential geographic extent of harmful ashfall were not available at the time of ingestion into the dataset. The buffer method used is an overly simplistic approach to an inherently complex task of modelling Ashfall impacts.  While the U.S. Geological Survey’s National Volcanic Threat Layer does provide a breakdown between proximal volcanic hazards and lahars, Ashfall is not included in the v

f modelling Ashfall impacts.  While the U.S. Geological Survey’s National Volcanic Threat Layer does provide a breakdown between proximal volcanic hazards and lahars, Ashfall is not included in the volcanic threat potential. This is a major volcanic hazard that has the potential to spread far beyond the volcanic eruption source, wherever that volcanic event may occur.  Limited eruption history data for nearly all volcanoes within the United States and its territories limits the possibility of calculating a volcano-specific recurrence interval. The nature of volcanic eruptions also limits the accuracy, should a recurrence interval be provided for a subset of the volcano list.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 22-1 22. Wildfire A Wildfire is an unplanned fire burning in natural or wildland areas, such as forest, shrub lands, grasslands, or prairies. The Wildfire hazard was developed using probabilistic data from the US Forest Service to calculate frequency of hazard events, susceptible areas, and impacts. As these data are not available for the Territories, the Wildfire hazard does not currently include American Samoa, Guam, Northern Mariana Islands, Puerto Rico, or U.S. Virgin Islands. The Wildfire hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 22.1. Wildfire Spatial Source Data Probabilistic Modeling and Susceptible Area Source: USDA, Forest Service, Spatial Datasets of Probabilistic Wildfire Risk Components for the United States139 The United States Forest Service Missoula

Probabilistic Modeling and Susceptible Area Source: USDA, Forest Service, Spatial Datasets of Probabilistic Wildfire Risk Components for the United States139 The United States Forest Service Missoula Fire Sciences Laboratory generated a series of raster datasets representing burn probability and conditional Flame Length Probability for the conterminous United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i through its geospatial Fire Simulation (FSim) system. FSim is designed to simulate the occurrence and growth of wildfires under tens of thousands of hypothetical contemporary fire seasons in order to estimate the probability of a given area (i.e., pixel) burning under 2024 landscape conditions and fire management practices. The burn probability raster dataset models the probability of an area being burned by a large fire (i.e., a fire that escapes initial fire suppression and spreads) at a 270-meter grid spatial resolution. The cell value in the raster file contains the mean annual burn probability as

by a large fire (i.e., a fire that escapes initial fire suppression and spreads) at a 270-meter grid spatial resolution. The cell value in the raster file contains the mean annual burn probability as a value between 0 and 1 and represents the tendency for the cell area to burn due to a large fire on an annual basis given its landscape, contemporary weather conditions, and probability of containment (see Figure 117). Flame-length probability is estimated for six standard Fire Intensity Levels and represents the likelihood of burning at a certain intensity level, given that a fire occurs. The Flame Length Probability (FLP) dataset consists of six raster files (one for each Fire Intensity Level), corresponding to the following flame length classes: FLP1 ≤ 2 ft; FLP2 = 2 < 4 ft.; FLP3 = 4 < 6 ft.; FLP4 = 6 < 8 ft.; FLP5 = 8 < 12 ft.; and FLP6 = 12+ ft. These files are also at a 270-meter grid spatial resolution. Note: Because burn probability and Flame Length Probability data are not avail

ft.; FLP4 = 6 < 8 ft.; FLP5 = 8 < 12 ft.; and FLP6 = 12+ ft. These files are also at a 270-meter grid spatial resolution. Note: Because burn probability and Flame Length Probability data are not available for American Samoa, Guam, the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, Puerto Rico, or the U.S. Virgin Islands, exposure, annualized frequency, and, therefore, Expected Annual Loss cannot be computed for these territories. 139 Short, Karen C.; Finney, Mark A.; Vogler, Kevin C.; Scott, Joe H.; Gilbertson-Day, Julie W.; Grenfell, Isaac C. (2024). Spatial datasets of probabilistic wildfire risk components for the United States (270m). 3rd Edition. Fort Collins, CO: Forest Service Research Data Archive. https://doi.org/10.2737/RDS-2016-0034-3.

The tool uses the attributes of the raster cell (see Table 83) to directly calculate area- weighted exposure and frequency for both developed area and agriculture area within each Census block. DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Every county covered by the Wildfire probability raster had at least some possibility of Wildfire occurrence, so all counties were deemed possible for Wildfire occurrence. While the current data source does not supply information for American Samoa, Guam, Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, Puerto Rico or the U.S. Virgin Islands, these United States territories are still included as possible for Wildfire occurrence. No Scores are available for the United States territories as the data are insufficient. 22.3. Wildfire Exposure Areas deemed susceptible to Wildfire are defined for each consequence type as the Census block developed area or agriculture area where the BP is greater than 0 and the modeled possibility of large fires reaching a Fire Intensity Level at or above a given threshold is greater than 0. Agricultural area and a Fire Intensity Level thresho

he BP is greater than 0 and the modeled possibility of large fires reaching a Fire Intensity Level at or above a given threshold is greater than 0. Agricultural area and a Fire Intensity Level threshold corresponding to FLP4 (flame lengths of 6-8ft) is used for the agriculture consequence type to determine exposure area. To determine exposure area for the population consequence type, the Census block developed area and a FIL threshold corresponding to FLP4 is used. For the building consequence type, exposure area is determined using the Census block developed area and a Fire Intensity Level threshold corresponding to FLP5 (flame lengths of 8- 12ft). To identify areas of exposure, the Wildfire raster cells are intersected with the Census block developed area and agriculture area polygons utilizing the custom raster-vector intersect tool. Wildfire Annualized Frequency The annualized frequency value represents the area-weighted burn probability (due to a large fire) of a location in a given year. The annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level (see Equation 143), and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations for a Census block were performed within the same custom developed raster-vector tool used to calculate exposure. The tool output provides the area-weighted annualized frequency of Wildfire probability, where the area is defined as the Census block’s developed area or agriculture area. Note that, unlike the frequency calculations

ovides the area-weighted annualized frequency of Wildfire probability, where the area is defined as the Census block’s developed area or agriculture area. Note that, unlike the frequency calculations for other hazards, the Wildfire frequency is consequence-dependent, so agriculture frequency may differ from the building and population frequency. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 22-15 The Expected Annual Loss Score represents a community’s national percentile ranking relative to all communities at the same level. Building Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Wildfire Expected Annual Loss for building by the total building value of the community. Population Expected Annual Loss Rate is calculated by dividing the Wildfire Expected Annual Loss for population by the total population of the community. Winter Weather Winter Weather consists of winter storm events in which the main types of precipitation are snow, sleet, or freezing rain. The Winter Weather hazard modeling uses Mesonet data to provide impact areas for this hazard that are derived from the National Weather Service’s warnings and advisories. SHELDUS data were used for historical losses for Winter Weather events and generate the Historic Loss Ratios. These are overlaid with the exposure values provided in Hazus 6.0 for Building, Population, and Agriculture for the final Expected Annual Loss. The Winter Weather hazard calculates risk for all three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture. For a summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, please see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 23.1. Winter Weather Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Compiling Source: Iowa State University, Iowa Environmental Mesonet141 The Nati

see Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison. 23.1. Winter Weather Spatial Source Data Historical Occurrence Compiling Source: Iowa State University, Iowa Environmental Mesonet141 The National Weather Service is continuously issuing weather alerts based on current weather conditions. 142 Each alert is coded by type of phenomenon and level of significance, (e.g., Watch, Warning, or Advisory) and conceptually can indicate the potential for weather event activities in a specific area. Archived National Weather Service alerts are aggregated, continuously updated, and made available for download in shapefile format by Iowa State University's Iowa Environmental Mesonet. Data include geometry for each alert's issued area and attributes related to each alert’s severity and phenomena type. Weather alerts are also timestamped with the time of issuance and the time of expiration. A table describing this dataset’s attributes can be found in App endix C – Mesonet-National Weather Servic

pe. Weather alerts are also timestamped with the time of issuance and the time of expiration. A table describing this dataset’s attributes can be found in App endix C – Mesonet-National Weather Service Weather Event Attribute Description. The geometry shape for each alert record represents the geographic area for which the National Weather Service alert applied. However, the Mesonet shapes are simplified versions of the more detailed National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shape originally associated with the alert record. Because the Mesonet tabular data still retains the reference IDs for the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones, these IDs are able to be used to relate to the more detailed zone associated with each alert record. The National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones can be downloaded in shapefile format143 and represent the codified areas for which weather alerts are issued by National Weather Service. The Public Forecast Zones shape definitions are predo

t Zones can be downloaded in shapefile format143 and represent the codified areas for which weather alerts are issued by National Weather Service. The Public Forecast Zones shape definitions are predominantly derived from county boundaries. While the Public Forecast Zone boundaries are more refined than those substituted into the Mesonet data, they are not at the same resolution as the current county boundaries derived from Census blocks. 141 Department of Agronomy, Iowa State University. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 23-2 Utilizing the Public Forecast Zone shapefile in conjunction with the Public Forecast Zone – County Correlation file, 144 a determination was made as to which Public Forecast Zones have single-county coverage and which are either sub-county zones or made of portions of multiple counties. For perspective, the following approximate distributions of forecast zone composition were found:  70% of the zones have single-county coverage.  20% are cases where a single county is subdivided into multiple zones.  10% are zones that breach parts of multiple contiguous counties. For tho se Forecast Zones covering a single county, the United States Census Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing 2021 county boundaries (Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing 2024 boundaries for Connecticut) are substituted. Another aspect of the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones is that they can and

opologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing 2024 boundaries for Connecticut) are substituted. Another aspect of the National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones is that they can and have changed over time. In the Mesonet data (2005 through 2024), there are many distinct Forecast Zones referenced that do not exist in the current National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shapefile. This occurs when a National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone has been modified in shape, renamed, and/or “retired” from use. Further research found that the National Weather Service maintains a downloadable Change History log of the various changes in Forecast Zone areas since 1997. This text file does not contain the pre- nor post-shape of the altered forecast zone. Archived versions of these changes are likely available via contact with National Weather Service, but the effort to match the National Weather Service issued alert record to the version-controlled shape representation of

these changes are likely available via contact with National Weather Service, but the effort to match the National Weather Service issued alert record to the version-controlled shape representation of the forecast zone at the time of alert issue seems to be beyond the scope of the processing effort, though a Mesonet representative was contacted to see if Forecast Zone shapes associated with each year of alert data had been archived. Unfortunately, no such archival information was available. For cases where the more refined National Weather Service Forecast Zone shape is unavailable, the simplified Mesonet boundary version shape is used. Winter Weather occurrences are measured in event-days as this more accurately represents the variability of Winter Weather event

ingle county, a better resolution version of the county boundary area. Winter Weather occurrences are measured in event-days as this more accurately represents the variability of Winter Weather event duration. To capture this, each native alert record with a duration greater than a single day was replaced with multiple records, one for each day of the original record’s duration. If a Winter Weather event’s duration on any given day was less than 6 hours, then the event was assigned to the day having the greatest duration of the event. This handled cases where the event occurs in the late evening and actually endured for a greater length of time on the next calendar day than on the day the alert was issued. For cases where the event duration is longer, the following logic was used: If a weather event’s duration was greater than 6 hours, assign the event to all days on which 6 or more hours occur. For example, if a 14-hour weather event was issued for 2 AM until 6 PM on January 1, then t

ther event’s duration was greater than 6 hours, assign the event to all days on which 6 or more hours occur. For example, if a 14-hour weather event was issued for 2 AM until 6 PM on January 1, then the event would be assigned to January 1. If the alert was issued from 11 PM on January 1 to 1 PM on January 2, then the event would be assigned to only January 2. If the alert was issued from 7 PM on January 1 to 9 AM on January 2, then the event would be assigned to both January 1 and January 2. To illustrate this concept, the Winter Weather occurrences in Table 88 are expanded to create the Winter Weather event-day records in Table 89. Additionally, there were some data quality issues with the Mesonet data. For example, some warnings had an expiration date that is prior to the issue date. In these cases, a single record was used and assigned the issue date.

DETERMINATION OF POSSIBILITY OF HAZARD OCCURRENCE Winter Weather can occur almost anywhere in the United States as the definition of a Winter Weather occurrence is locally defined by the area’s weather forecast office. For example, a forecast office in Texas may define a Winter Weather occurrence differently than a forecast office in New York. Therefore, all counties were deemed possible for Winter Weather occurrence. PERIOD OF RECORD The National Weather Service’s system of recording WWA in the 1990s and early 2000s was too difficult for automated processing. In 2005 the Valid Time Extent Code system was implemented, which allowed for the easy automated parsing of alert data. Therefore, National Weather Service weather events data were downloaded for 2005 through 2024. The date range is Nov. 12, 2005 to Nov. 12, 2024, so the period of record for which Winter Weather data are utilized is 19.0 years. 23.3. Winter Weather Non-Spatial Data Processing 23.3.1. HISTORICAL OCCURRENCE COUNT The historical occurrence count of Winter Weather, in event-days, is computed as the area-weighted sum of distinct Winter Weather event-day polygons that intersect a Census block. This count uses the same Winter Weather expansion Census block intersection table used to find exposure at the Census block level and are used to compute annualized frequenc

intersect a Census block. This count uses the same Winter Weather expansion Census block intersection table used to find exposure at the Census block level and are used to compute annualized frequency at the Census block level. Historical event-day counts are also supplied at the Census tract and county levels as the number of distinct Winter Weather event-day polygons that intersect the Census tract and county, respectively. 23.4. Winter Weather Exposure To identify areas of exposure, the Winter Weather event-day polygons (also referred to as Winter Storm Date Expansions to acknowledge the spatiotemporal processing described in Section 23.2 Winter Weather Spatial Data Processing) are intersected with the Census block polygons within the processing database. The resulting table contains the Winter Weather event-day’s unique identifier, Census block number, and the intersected area in square kilometers (see Table 90). National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 23-8 Because the exposure model uses a conservative-case concentration of exposure and a developed area density value, it is possible to mathematically generate an exposure value that is greater than the total value of the Census block. The Hazus-recorded population and building value and the Census of Agriculture-reported crop and livestock value for the Census block are considered ceilings on exposure. For example, if the calculated exposed building value exceeds the Hazus-recorded building value, then the Hazus-recorded building value is used as the building exposure value for the Census block. 23.4.1. Winter Weather Annualized Frequency The number of recorded Winter Weather occurrences, in event-days, each year over the period of record (19.0 years) is used to estimate the annualized frequency of Winter Weather events in an area. Because a Winter Weather event can last over several days or a single day, an event-day basis was used to estimate annualized frequency as this method better captures the variability in duration between occurrences. The annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level using Equation 150, and the Census block-level value is u

this method better captures the variability in duration between occurrences. The annualized frequency is calculated at the Census block level using Equation 150, and the Census block-level value is used in the Expected Annual Loss calculations. Annualized frequency calculations use the same intersection between Winter Weather event-days (or Winter Storm Date Expansion) polygons and Census block polygons that were used to calculate exposure. The count of distinct Winter Weather event-day polygons intersecting each Census block is recorded and used to calculate the annualized frequency of Winter Weather event-days.

An annual rate is calculated as the event-day count divided by the period of record of 19.0 years, and this rate is multiplied by the SHELDUS period of record of 28 years to estimate a historical event-day count for the appropriate time range. If the number of loss-causing Winter Weather event-day records from SHELDUS is less than the scaled event-day count for the county, then a number of zero-loss records equal to the difference are inserted into the Loss Ratio per Basis table with zero values for the consequence ratios. Winter Weather Methodology Discussion This section briefly presents some of the limitations of the current methodology and data, implications of the current methodology and datasets, and considerations for using the data provided to the user. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  National Weather Service Warnings and Advisories are issued at the discretion of each individual Na

. See Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations for non-hazard-specific assumptions and limitations.  National Weather Service Warnings and Advisories are issued at the discretion of each individual National Weather Service Forecast Office, based on regionally determined criteria. Though this accounts for varying capacities across the country, it results in an inconsistent record of dangerous winter weather events.  The data development team made several decisions regarding the delineation between what is considered Winter Weather versus other cold-based hazards. These have been outlined in this Technical Document.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 Appendix D-1 Appendix D – Fishnet Occurrence Count Table 112: Sample Historic Fishnet Hazard Event/Event-Day Count Data For wid espread hazards that can occur anywhere within a county, a historic occurrence count (event or event-day) is performed at the level of a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid cell (see Table 112) which is then intersected with the Census block, Census tract, or county to estimate annualized frequency. If a Census block, Census tract, or county intersects multiple fishnet grid cells (see Figure 130), an area-weighted average count is calculated. For example, the reference Census block below is intersected by four fishnet cells, each of which intersects a different count of occurrence event (or event-day) polygons. The hazard occurrence count for this Census block would be calculated according to Equation 155 and aggregated to the Census tract and county levels according to Equation 156. 146 Tornado event counts are a

zard occurrence count for this Census block would be calculated according to Equation 155 and aggregated to the Census tract and county levels according to Equation 156. 146 Tornado event counts are actually performed for each EF-scale and follow a different methodology using the fishnet grid cell counts. See the Tornado frequency documentation for more information. National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 Appendix E-1 Appendix E – Social Vulnerability Index Technical Documentation for processing the Social Vulnerability data obtained from the Census Community Resilience Estimates team. This document describes how the Social Vulnerability data obtained from Census is used to calculate the Social Vulnerability scores used in the National Risk Index risk calculation. Overview This document describes how community-level inputs provided by the U.S. Census are transformed into:  a dominance count using a one–standard deviation (~68%) interval, and  a corresponding percentile score based on that dominance count. The resu lting percentile score is the Social Vulnerability value used in the FEMA National Risk Index risk calculation and provided in the data downloads. The method incorporates the Census-provided uncertainty around each raw percentile by converting the published 90% confidence interval to an approximate 1 standard deviatio

ided in the data downloads. The method incorporates the Census-provided uncertainty around each raw percentile by converting the published 90% confidence interval to an approximate 1 standard deviation interval via a normal approximation, as recommended by Census for this use case. Why use 1 standard deviation instead of 90% Confidence Interval? Census ad vised that a 1 standard deviation interval better differentiates communities (the 90% interval produces too many ties), and agreed the normal approximation used to obtain the standard error is reasonable for this application. Input Data For each community, we use the following Census-provided values: Raw percentile score The base Social Vulnerability percentile for the community (0–100). Higher values indicate higher relative vulnerability. 90% confidence interval lower bound The lower bound of the 90% confidence interval for the raw percentile score. 90% confidence interval upper bound The upper bound of the 90% confidence interval f

y. 90% confidence interval lower bound The lower bound of the 90% confidence interval for the raw percentile score. 90% confidence interval upper bound The upper bound of the 90% confidence interval for the raw percentile score.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 Appendix E-2 Note: The 90% interval bounds are produced by Census using a bootstrap with underlying survey data. Method Summary  Derive an estimated standard error from the 90% interval using a normal approximation, then form a 1 standard deviation interval around the raw percentile.  Compute a dominance count for each community: the number of other communities that are clearly less vulnerable using the 1 standard deviation intervals.  Convert dominance counts to a percentile scale to produce the final Social Vulnerability percentile used by the National Risk Index. STEP 1 — DERIVE THE 1 STANDARD DEVIATION INTERVAL FROM THE 90% INTERVAL Under a normal approximation, a two-sided 90% confidence interval can be expressed as: (point estimate) ± 1.645 × (standard error). Therefore, we estimate the standard error as: standard error = (upper 90% bound − lower 90% bound) / (2 × 1.645) We then form a one–standard deviation interval (a

estimate) ± 1.645 × (standard error). Therefore, we estimate the standard error as: standard error = (upper 90% bound − lower 90% bound) / (2 × 1.645) We then form a one–standard deviation interval (approximately 68% coverage) around the raw percentile: 1 standard deviation lower bound = raw percentile − standard error 1 standard deviation upper bound = raw percentile + standard error STEP 2 — DOMINANCE COUNTING (USING 1 STANDARD DEVIATION INTERVALS) For each community A, we compare its 1 standard deviation lower bound to the 1 standard deviation upper bounds of all other communities B: A is considered more vulnerable than B (with ~68% confidence) if: (A’s 1 standard deviation lower bound) ≥ (B’s 1 standard deviation upper bound)  The dominance count for a community is the total number of other communities for which this condition holds.  Self-comparisons are excluded.  A dominance proportion can be computed as the dominance count divided by (total communities − 1). Intuiti on: If t

other communities for which this condition holds.  Self-comparisons are excluded.  A dominance proportion can be computed as the dominance count divided by (total communities − 1). Intuiti on: If the lower edge of A’s plausible range is at least as high as the upper edge of B’s plausible range, then A’s vulnerability is clearly higher than B’s at the 1 standard deviation level.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 Appendix E-3 STEP 3 — CONVERT DOMINANCE COUNTS TO PERCENTILE SCORES To produce the final Social Vulnerability percentile score used by the National Risk Index, we rank communities by their 1 standard deviation dominance counts and convert those ranks to percentiles. For a given community, this is interpreted as the percentage of communities with an equal or lower 1 standard deviation dominance count. This transformation places the result on a familiar 0–100 scale and helps align the output with the broader National Risk Index percentile framework. Output  1 standard deviation dominance count — number of other communities that are less vulnerable than a community at the ~68% (1 standard deviation) level.  Social Vulnerability percentile (based on 1 standard deviation dominance) — the percentile rank of the 1 standard deviation dominance count among all communities; this is the score used in National Risk Index risk calculations

ntile (based on 1 standard deviation dominance) — the percentile rank of the 1 standard deviation dominance count among all communities; this is the score used in National Risk Index risk calculations and provided in data downloads. Interpretation  A higher 1 standard deviation dominance count indicates a community clearly exceeds more peers in vulnerability when accounting for uncertainty using the 1 standard deviation interval.  The final percentile reflects a community’s position relative to all others on this “clear vulnerability” dimension; for example, a value of 85 means the community’s 1 standard deviation dominance count is greater than or equal to 85% of communities. Reasoning  Use of 1 standard deviation intervals: Recommended by Census to reduce ties and increase differentiation among communities.  Normal approximation for standard error: Recommended by Census for converting the published 90% interval into a standard error for the 1 standard deviation interval.  Domina

tion among communities.  Normal approximation for standard error: Recommended by Census for converting the published 90% interval into a standard error for the 1 standard deviation interval.  Dominance definition: Comparing the lower bound of one community to the upper bound of another ensures a “clear separation” in vulnerability given uncertainty. Assumptions and Limitations  The conversion from the 90% interval to a standard error and then to a 1 standard deviation interval assumes approximate normality of the raw percentile estimates provided by Census.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 Appendix E-4  The 90% interval bounds originate from a bootstrap performed by Census on underlying survey responses that are not publicly available. We treat those intervals as valid summaries of uncertainty.

National Risk Index Technical Documentation December 2025 Appendix F-1 Appendix F – Community Resilience Variable Gathering: Technical Documentation Overview This document provides comprehensive technical documentation for the 12 community resilience variables derived from U.S. Census American Community Survey (ACS) data and 3 variables derived from the U.S. Census Community Business Patterns data. Each variable represents an input into the Social or Economic Pillars of the HVRI BRIC. Each pillar must be recalculated to align with the new direction of FEMA and National Risk Index Data. These data were used, along with data from the LEAD tool and County Health Rankings, to recalculate the Baseline Resilience Indicators for Communities index with variables pertaining to food security, racial/ethnic income inequality, and gender income inequality removed.
